proposals.title: Robust Source Separation for Real-World Music Mixtures via Latent Diffusion and Learned Fingerprints
proposals.motivation: Current audio source separation models are often trained and evaluated on synthetic or limited datasets, failing to generalize to the complex, noisy, and highly overlapping sources found in real-world music recordings. This limits their practical utility for music production, remixing, and music information retrieval tasks. Extending separation frameworks to richer, real-world datasets is a critical step toward building robust, production-ready audio AI tools.
proposals.hypothesis: A separation framework combining a diffusion-based generative model with a robust, pre-trained audio fingerprinting model (like a music foundation model) can effectively disentangle and reconstruct individual instrument sources from complex, real-world music mixtures where supervised training data is scarce.
proposals.method: We will adapt the SENSE (Source Separation with Example Guidance) framework, which uses a latent diffusion model conditioned on a source embedding. Instead of simple embeddings, we will condition on rich, time-aligned feature representations extracted from a pre-2026 music foundation model (e.g., Jukebox embeddings or a model from the Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting paper). The model will be trained on a dataset of real-world, multi-track music recordings (e.g., MUSDB18-HQ or MedleyDB) to learn the mapping from a mixture spectrogram and a target source's fingerprint to a separated source spectrogram. We will employ the adversarial and reconstruction losses used in prior separation work to ensure fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a state-of-the-art model for separating real-world music mixtures, moving beyond clean, synthetic benchmarks. It will provide a rigorous evaluation of music foundation model features for conditioning generative audio tasks. The resulting open-source model and benchmarks will serve as a valuable resource for both the MIR and generative audio communities.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models have been applied to separation, their conditioning on deep, pre-trained musical representations for real-world data is unexplored. This uniquely bridges John Thickstun's prior work on audio fingerprinting and generative models (SENSE, Aria) to solve a core, unsolved problem in music audio processing.

proposals.title: Controlled Audio Augmentation via Attribute-Conditioned Diffusion-LM for Music Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Music generation and audio understanding models require large, diverse datasets, but real-world data is often imbalanced in attributes like genre, instrumentation, or structure. This scarcity limits model robustness and controllability, a key challenge in pre-2026 music AI. Synthetic data augmentation can address this, but existing audio augmentation techniques lack fine-grained, semantic control over the generated content.
proposals.hypothesis: A controllable Diffusion-LM, fine-tuned for audio, can generate high-fidelity, attribute-specific synthetic music data that, when used for augmentation, will improve the performance and attribute-conditioning capabilities of downstream music foundation models on underrepresented classes.
proposals.method: We will adapt the pre-2026 Diffusion-LM architecture, a diffusion-based language model known for its controllability, to the symbolic music domain using a tokenizer like the one from the Hookpad Aria system. The model will be conditioned on specific musical attributes (e.g., chord progression complexity, rhythmic density) derived from the FACTUAL/MUSICAL evaluation framework. We will generate synthetic data targeting underrepresented attribute combinations and use it to augment training sets for a music foundation model, evaluating gains on attribute-specific generation and retrieval tasks using the robust fingerprinting and evaluation metrics from Thickstun's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel methodology for controllable, semantic audio data augmentation, directly addressing dataset bias in music AI. It will yield a publicly available, attribute-conditioned symbolic music generator and demonstrate measurable performance improvements in downstream models on tasks requiring fine-grained musical control.
proposals.novelty: While diffusion models are used for audio generation, their application as a *controllable data augmentation engine* for music foundation models is unexplored. This approach uniquely combines the attribute-level controllability of Diffusion-LM with the domain-specific evaluation and tokenization frameworks from Thickstun's recent research.

proposals.title: Controllable Multi-Modal Synthesis via a Continuous Latent Interface for Text, Image, and Music
proposals.motivation: Current generative models for text, image, and audio operate largely in isolation, with limited frameworks for joint, fine-grained control across modalities. This hinders the creation of cohesive, semantically aligned multi-media content, such as a song with corresponding lyrics and album art. Building on the success of continuous latent spaces in music generation (e.g., ARIA) and diffusion models for images/text, a unified control mechanism is a critical next step for creative AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A shared, continuous latent framework, informed by cross-modal attention and contrastive learning, can enable precise, joint control over the semantic and stylistic attributes of generated text, images, and music.
proposals.method: We will extend the continuous latent control paradigm from Hookpad ARIA to a multi-modal setting. A transformer-based encoder will project text prompts, image features (from a pre-trained CLIP/ViT), and music embeddings (from a music foundation model) into a shared latent space. This space will be regularized via a contrastive loss (e.g., CLIP-style) to align representations across modalities. For generation, this latent vector will condition a pipeline of pre-trained or fine-tuned diffusion models (Stable Diffusion for images, a music language model like Jukebox or a diffusion audio model for music, and a language model for text) via cross-attention, allowing a single control vector to steer all outputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a practical framework for synchronized multi-modal generation, moving beyond single-modality tools. It will introduce novel evaluation metrics, extending the researcher's prior work on factual/musical evaluation to assess cross-modal coherence. The resulting model and code will serve as a foundation for future interactive creative assistants.
proposals.novelty: While cross-modal alignment exists (e.g., CLIP), and controllable generation exists within single modalities, jointly controlling the synthesis of text, image, and music from a unified continuous latent space is unexplored. The key innovation is applying the 'continuous latent control' concept from music generation to a tri-modal setting, creating a new type of creative interface.

proposals.title: Structural Music Language Modeling: Integrating Long-Term Context for Coherent Audio Generation
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models, including autoregressive Music Language Models (MLMs), often operate on short, frame-level audio tokens, limiting their ability to model high-level musical structure like form, repetition, and development. This leads to compositions that lack long-term coherence and fail to exhibit the narrative arcs and sectional relationships central to compelling music. Addressing this gap is critical for moving from generating plausible short phrases to creating complete, structurally sound musical works.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling long-term dependencies and structural relationships within a hierarchical Music Language Model will significantly improve the global coherence, formal consistency, and subjective quality of generated musical audio compared to frame-based baselines.
proposals.method: We will extend the Jukebox/ARIA-style autoregressive framework by introducing a two-tier hierarchical transformer. A high-level 'structure transformer' will operate on a compressed, semantic representation of musical segments (e.g., 4-bar chunks), predicting the sequence of sections (verse, chorus, bridge). This structural context will condition a lower-level 'audio transformer' that generates the detailed frame-level audio tokens (e.g., EnCodec tokens). We will train this model on large-scale, symbolically aligned audio datasets (like Slakh) to learn the mapping between structural labels and audio, and evaluate using the factual and musical metrics from Thickstun's prior work, augmented with new structural consistency measures.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel hierarchical MLM architecture for long-context music audio generation, 2) a systematic framework for evaluating structural coherence in generated music, and 3) open-source models and code that demonstrate a measurable advance in the production of globally coherent musical compositions.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in MLMs has focused on frame-level prediction or short-term conditioning (e.g., lead sheets), our approach uniquely integrates an explicit, trainable structural planning module directly into the audio generation pipeline, enabling top-down compositional control informed by learned musical form.

proposals.title: Beyond Pitch: Modeling Expressive Performance Variations for Robust Music Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current data augmentation for music AI, especially in audio fingerprinting and generation, often relies heavily on simple label-preserving pitch shifts. However, real-world musical performances vary more richly in expressive dimensions like micro-timing, dynamics, and articulation. Ignoring these variations limits model robustness and the realism of generated audio. This problem is critical for building reliable music foundation models and assistive tools like Hookpad Aria.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that augmenting training data with synthesized, label-preserving variations in timing and dynamics, modeled on real performance data, will significantly improve the robustness and generalization of music representation models compared to augmentation based on pitch shift alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage the Jukebox architecture or a similar music foundation model as a feature extractor to analyze a dataset of multiple performances of the same musical piece (e.g., from MIDI datasets or aligned audio). Using techniques from the SENSE framework for structured latent spaces, we will learn disentangled representations of score-aligned content (pitch, rhythm) and performance style (timing, velocity). We will then use these learned distributions to programmatically generate realistic, label-preserving augmentations for training datasets, which will be used to train and evaluate models on tasks from Thickstun's prior work, such as neural audio fingerprinting and music language model evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a principled, data-driven method for generating realistic musical augmentations, moving beyond simple audio effects. It will deliver new benchmarks for evaluating model robustness to performance nuances and likely improve the performance of downstream tasks like audio matching and conditional music generation.
proposals.novelty: While pitch/time-stretch are standard, modeling nuanced, correlated performance variations (e.g., a rallentando with a crescendo) as a structured, label-preserving augmentation space is novel. This integrates performance modeling from symbolic music research directly into the augmentation pipeline for audio-based deep learning.

proposals.title: End-to-End Learnable Filterbanks: Joint Optimization with Neural Architecture Search for Audio Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Audio foundation models for music rely heavily on the initial time-frequency representation, typically a fixed, hand-designed filterbank like Mel-spectrograms. These representations are suboptimal for specific downstream tasks and do not leverage the power of end-to-end learning. Jointly optimizing the front-end filterbank with the neural network architecture could yield more efficient and task-specific audio representations, improving performance in music generation, fingerprinting, and analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly optimizing the parameters of a learnable filterbank (e.g., a parametric or neural filter layer) with the architecture of a subsequent convolutional neural network (CNN) via Neural Architecture Search (NAS) will produce a system superior to fixed-filterbank baselines on music understanding and generation tasks.
proposals.method: We will implement a learnable filterbank front-end, such as a 1D convolutional layer with constrained, interpretable filters or a learnable gammatone-like parameterization. This will feed into a CNN search space defined by operations (depthwise-sep conv, dilated conv), kernel sizes, and skip connections. We will employ a differentiable NAS method (e.g., DARTS) to jointly optimize the filterbank parameters and the CNN architecture weights end-to-end on tasks from Thickstun's prior work, such as audio fingerprinting robustness or music language model evaluation.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel, fully learnable audio front-end framework tailored for music. It will provide empirical evidence on the benefits of end-to-end filterbank learning for music AI and release optimized architectures that could serve as improved feature extractors for next-generation music foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While learnable filterbanks and NAS have been explored separately in generic audio/speech, their joint, task-specific optimization for music tasks is novel. This approach moves beyond tuning a fixed CNN on fixed features to co-designing the feature extraction and processing network.

proposals.title: Evaluating the Polyphonic and Cross-Cultural Robustness of Music Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current music language models (MLMs) are predominantly trained and evaluated on Western popular music, which often features simple harmonic structures and sparse textures. This creates a significant gap in understanding model performance on musically complex or culturally diverse material, limiting their global applicability and raising concerns about representational bias in generative AI for music. Assessing robustness on dense polyphony and non-Western traditions is a critical step toward building more general and equitable music AI systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that state-of-the-art MLMs, such as those based on the SENSE or ARIA architectures, will exhibit a significant performance drop on tasks involving dense polyphonic textures and music from non-Western traditions, revealing specific failure modes related to harmonic density and cultural musical grammar.
proposals.method: We will extend the evaluation framework from 'Factual and Musical Evaluation Metrics for Music Language Models' to new benchmark datasets. These will include the Bach Chorales and MAESTRO for complex Western polyphony, and the Dunya datasets for Turkish Makam and Carnatic music. Using the pre-trained models from the 'Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting' and 'Hookpad ARIA' projects, we will conduct controlled experiments measuring standard metrics (e.g., perplexity, chord accuracy) and novel, culturally-aware metrics for tasks like continuation, infilling, and transcription across these datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic benchmark of MLM robustness across polyphonic and cross-cultural domains, identifying specific architectural or data limitations. It will contribute a new, open-source evaluation suite and actionable insights for dataset curation and model design to improve the generality of next-generation music AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work evaluates MLMs on held-out Western pop, a rigorous, multi-dimensional evaluation targeting both structural complexity (polyphony) and cultural specificity (non-Western traditions) is novel. This moves beyond simple accuracy to diagnose model failures in musically meaningful dimensions.

proposals.title: Structured Self-Attention for Hierarchical Music Generation and Analysis
proposals.motivation: Current symbolic music models, including autoregressive transformers, often struggle to capture the complex, multi-scale hierarchical structure of music, where local motifs form phrases, sections, and entire pieces. This limits their ability to generate coherent long-form compositions and to perform structured musical analysis. A method that explicitly models these long-range dependencies across different musical timescales is needed to advance music AI beyond short-term pattern completion.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a transformer architecture employing a structured, multi-resolution self-attention mechanism, inspired by hierarchical NLP models, can more effectively capture the long-range dependencies and hierarchical organization of symbolic music than standard full-sequence attention, leading to improved performance in generation and analysis tasks.
proposals.method: We will develop a transformer-based model for symbolic music (e.g., MIDI or REMI-like token sequences). The core innovation is a structured attention mechanism that operates at multiple resolutions: a local windowed attention for note-to-note relationships, a dilated or pooled attention for phrase-level structure, and a sparse global attention for sectional form. This architecture will be trained on large-scale symbolic music datasets (e.g., Lakh MIDI) for next-token prediction. We will evaluate it against baseline transformers on the factual and musical metrics established in Thickstun's prior work, focusing on long-range coherence, structure, and repetition.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel neural architecture for symbolic music that explicitly addresses musical hierarchy. It will provide new benchmarks for long-form musical coherence and structure in generation. The model and its attention patterns could also serve as a tool for computational music analysis, revealing how networks internalize musical form.
proposals.novelty: While transformers are used in Music Language Models (MLMs), applying structured, hierarchical attention schemes from NLP (e.g., Longformer, BigBird) to symbolic music is novel. This moves beyond treating music as a flat token sequence and directly encodes inductive biases about musical multi-scale structure into the model's core mechanism.

proposals.title: Dilated Convolutional Context for Efficient Music Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current Music Language Models (MLMs) and audio generation models, such as those evaluated in Thickstun's recent work, rely heavily on transformer architectures or standard convolutions for temporal modeling. These can be computationally expensive or limited in receptive field, hindering the efficient capture of long-range musical structure (e.g., verse-chorus form, thematic development). Efficiently modeling multi-scale temporal dependencies is crucial for coherent, long-form music generation and analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: Incorporating dilated convolutions into the temporal modeling layers of music foundation models will enable more efficient and effective capture of multi-scale musical context, leading to improved performance on long-range generation and analysis tasks without a proportional increase in parameters or compute.
proposals.method: We will design and integrate dilated convolutional blocks into the encoder and/or decoder of a transformer-based Music Language Model (e.g., a model from the Jukebox or MusicLM lineage). The blocks will use exponentially increasing dilation rates to create a large receptive field over audio tokens or latent representations. This hybrid architecture will be trained and evaluated on the tasks of conditional music continuation and music audio fingerprinting (extending the 'Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting' work), using the factual and musical metrics established in prior research to measure improvements in long-range coherence and structural awareness.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, computationally efficient architectural component for music AI, specifically improving the modeling of long-term structure. It will yield a publicly available model and a comparative analysis demonstrating the trade-offs between dilated convolutions and pure attention for musical sequence modeling.
proposals.novelty: While dilated convolutions are established in raw audio synthesis (e.g., WaveNet), their application to the discrete token or latent space of modern, large-scale music foundation models for the explicit purpose of capturing hierarchical musical form is unexplored. This bridges a proven technique from audio synthesis with the state-of-the-art paradigm of music language modeling.

proposals.title: Adversarially Robust Audio Transcription: Improving Music Language Model Resilience to Real-World Distortions
proposals.motivation: Music transcription models, including audio-to-symbolic and lyric transcription systems, are brittle when faced with real-world audio distortions like background noise, compression artifacts, or reverb. This limits their practical utility in production and analysis of user-generated content. While adversarial training is a proven defense in computer vision, its application to audio models, particularly for complex musical signals, remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that incorporating adversarial examples, generated via Projected Gradient Descent (PGD) on the audio waveform or spectrogram input, during the training of music transcription models will significantly improve their robustness to a wide range of audio distortions without degrading performance on clean audio.
proposals.method: We will adapt the PGD adversarial training framework from vision to the audio domain. Starting with a pre-trained transcription model (e.g., a spectrogram-based encoder-decoder or a foundation model like Jukebox's encoder), we will generate adversarial perturbations bounded by an L-infinity norm in the input space. These adversarial examples, alongside standard data augmentations (e.g., noise, time-stretch), will be used to fine-tune the model. We will evaluate robustness using the FACT and MUSIC metrics from Thickstun's prior work, applied to distorted versions of standard datasets like MAESTRO or DAMP.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a robustified music transcription model and a benchmark for evaluating adversarial robustness in audio-to-symbolic tasks. It will provide the first systematic study of adversarial training's efficacy for complex, structured audio generation tasks like music transcription.
proposals.novelty: While adversarial robustness is well-studied in vision and speech ASR, applying it to the nuanced domain of music transcription—where the output is structured symbolic language (notes, chords, lyrics)—is novel. The integration of adversarial training with established musical evaluation metrics (FACT, MUSIC) for validation is a new contribution.

proposals.title: Generating Full Musical Scores with Hierarchical Transformers and Structured Latent Representations
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models, including frame-based audio models and symbolic music language models (MLMs), struggle to produce coherent, full-length songs with explicit, high-level musical structure (e.g., verses, choruses) and precise note-level timing. This limits their utility for professional songwriting and composition tools, where structured output is essential. Extending the capabilities of models like those used in Hookpad Aria to generate complete, structured scores addresses a critical gap in controllable, high-fidelity music AI.
proposals.hypothesis: A hierarchical transformer architecture, conditioned on a structured latent representation of musical form, can generate coherent, multi-track symbolic musical scores with accurate note durations and measure-level alignment, outperforming existing frame-based or autoregressive token models on structural coherence and musicality.
proposals.method: We will extend the frame-based generation paradigm from prior work (e.g., SENSE) to a two-stage hierarchical model. First, a high-level 'form transformer' will generate a sequence of latent tokens representing song sections (e.g., Intro, A, B, Chorus) and their repetitions, informed by user prompts or melodic hooks. Second, a conditioned 'note-duration transformer' will decode each section into a precise sequence of (pitch, duration, measure) tuples, using a novel relative positional encoding scheme to enforce metric regularity. Training will leverage large-scale symbolic datasets (e.g., Lakh MIDI) and evaluation will employ the factual and musical metrics from Thickstun's prior work, augmented with new structure-aware metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel model capable of generating complete, structured musical scores, a significant step beyond generating short loops or unsegmented sequences. It will provide a new benchmark for structural coherence in music generation and release a publicly available model and codebase to advance research in controllable symbolic music AI.
proposals.novelty: While prior work generates audio frames or token sequences, this proposal explicitly models musical form as a latent variable to guide the generation of note-level, metrically-aligned scores. This integration of high-level structure with low-level note-duration prediction in a single, end-to-trainable framework is a novel synthesis of ideas from music language modeling and latent variable models.

proposals.title: Inverse Transcription for Audio Synthesis: A Consistency-Guided Diffusion Approach
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models often produce audio that is musically incoherent or lacks precise structural control, limiting their utility for professional applications. High-quality symbolic-to-audio synthesis remains a significant challenge, especially for complex polyphonic music. This work addresses this gap by leveraging the robust symbolic representations learned by state-of-the-art transcription models as a prior for controllable audio generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using a pre-trained, robust neural audio transcription model as a consistency constraint within a diffusion-based audio synthesis framework will enable the generation of high-fidelity, musically faithful audio from symbolic transcriptions, outperforming end-to-end models in accuracy and structural coherence.
proposals.method: We will build upon a diffusion-based audio generation model (e.g., a latent diffusion model). The core innovation is a training and inference procedure that uses a frozen transcription model (inspired by the Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting work) as a feature extractor and consistency module. During training, we will enforce consistency between the model's generated spectrograms and their transcriptions via a perceptual loss in the transcription model's feature space. For inference, we will employ a sampling technique akin to classifier-free guidance, where the transcription model's output (e.g., a piano roll or embedding) steers the denoising process towards sequences that are both acoustically realistic and symbolically faithful.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a novel paradigm for controllable music synthesis that decouples symbolic understanding from acoustic modeling, leading to more precise and editable generation. It will provide a new benchmark for symbolic-to-audio synthesis quality and open avenues for applications like style transfer (by conditioning on both transcription and a style embedding) and intelligent audio editing.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses transcription models for analysis or as evaluation metrics, using one as a *direct, differentiable constraint* for training and guiding a generative audio model is novel. This inverts the typical analysis-by-synthesis pipeline into a synthesis-by-analysis framework.

proposals.title: Pitch-Invariant Audio Representations for Cross-Domain Source Separation and Diarization
proposals.motivation: Current speaker diarization and environmental sound separation systems often struggle with overlapping sources and pitch variation, relying on features not optimized for these specific challenges. Pre-2026, music foundation models have demonstrated robust, pitch-invariant representations for identifying musical works, suggesting these features could capture the timbral and rhythmic signatures essential for distinguishing non-musical sources. This project investigates the transfer of these powerful audio representations to critical speech and scene analysis tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pitch-invariant audio features, pre-trained on large-scale music data for tasks like fingerprinting, will provide superior representations for speaker diarization and environmental sound separation compared to standard spectral features or generic audio embeddings, due to their inherent robustness to pitch shifts and focus on timbral identity.
proposals.method: We will extract features from a pre-trained, robust neural audio fingerprinting model (like the one from Thickstun's prior work) which is designed to be invariant to pitch and tempo. These features will serve as input to a transformer or convolutional separation/diarization network. For diarization, we will adapt a clustering-based pipeline (e.g., using spectral clustering on the features). For separation, we will train a mask-prediction network (like a Conv-TasNet variant) conditioned on these features. Benchmarks will include the LibriMix dataset for separation and the AMI corpus for diarization.
proposals.contribution: This work will demonstrate a novel application of music-domain foundation models to general audio tasks, potentially improving state-of-the-art performance in separation and diarization. It will provide a framework for cross-domain audio representation transfer and yield new insights into the fundamental properties of audio embeddings useful for source discrimination.
proposals.novelty: While music models have been used for music tasks, their direct application to speaker diarization and environmental sound separation via pitch-invariant features is unexplored. The core novelty lies in repurposing a model trained for musical identity (fingerprinting) to solve the structurally similar problem of source identity in non-musical audio.

proposals.title: Self-Supervised Pre-Training of Translation-Invariant Audio Networks at Scale
proposals.motivation: Current audio foundation models, while powerful, often rely on supervised data or specific pre-training objectives that may not fully capture the rich, translation-invariant structures inherent in music and general audio. Scaling these models with massive, unlabeled audio corpora remains a critical challenge for achieving robust, general-purpose audio representations. This work addresses the need for scalable self-supervision that directly targets the temporal and pitch invariances crucial for music understanding and generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that pre-training a translation-invariant neural network architecture on a massive corpus of unlabeled audio using a novel self-supervised objective will yield audio embeddings that significantly outperform existing methods on downstream tasks like audio fingerprinting, music similarity, and conditional generation.
proposals.method: We will extend the translation-invariant network architecture from the 'Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting' work, designed to be equivariant to time-stretching and pitch-shifting. For self-supervision, we will employ a contrastive learning framework (e.g., SimCLR or BYOL) where positive pairs are created by applying random, realistic audio augmentations (time shifts, mild tempo changes, pitch shifts, and background noise) to segments from the same track. The model will be trained on a dataset of millions of hours of music and environmental audio. The pre-trained encoder will then be evaluated by fine-tuning on labeled benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a large-scale, self-supervised audio foundation model with built-in translation invariance, providing a powerful pre-trained backbone for music information retrieval and generation tasks. It will establish a new state-of-the-art for data-efficient fine-tuning on tasks like the ARIA hook generation system and the factual/musical evaluation of MLLMs. The work will also provide insights into which audio augmentations are most effective for learning musically meaningful representations.
proposals.novelty: While self-supervision is common in vision and NLP, its application to translation-invariant audio networks at this scale is novel. The key innovation is the co-design of a network architecture explicitly biased for musical transformations with a self-supervised objective that reinforces those invariances, moving beyond generic contrastive pre-training.

proposals.title: MAUVE-GUIDE: Direct Optimization of Music Language Models via MAUVE Gradient Guidance
proposals.motivation: Current music language models (MLMs) are trained primarily on next-token prediction, which does not directly optimize for high-level musical coherence or stylistic alignment with a target corpus. Pre-2026 evaluation work, such as the researcher's own, has established MAUVE as a robust metric for comparing distributions of generated and real music. However, its use has been limited to offline evaluation, not as a training signal for improving generation quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using the gradients of the MAUVE divergence as an auxiliary training signal can directly steer the parameters of a generative music model to produce outputs that are more coherent and stylistically consistent with a desired reference corpus, leading to measurable improvements over standard maximum likelihood training.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MAUVE algorithm, which quantifies divergence between text (or token sequence) distributions using embeddings from a pre-trained model, to provide gradients. For a baseline MLM (e.g., a decoder-only transformer), we will compute the MAUVE divergence between a batch of model-generated continuations and a reference dataset. We will then backpropagate through this computation, using the resulting gradient to update the MLM's parameters alongside the standard cross-entropy loss. This requires differentiating through the quantization and softmax steps of MAUVE's histogram construction. We will experiment with weighting schemes for the combined loss and evaluate on symbolic music datasets.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel training paradigm for MLMs that directly optimizes for distributional similarity, a high-level quality metric. It will provide the first method to use MAUVE proactively for model improvement rather than just retrospective evaluation. The resulting models are expected to generate more musically plausible and stylistically faithful sequences.
proposals.novelty: While MAUVE is a known evaluation metric, its direct integration as a differentiable training signal for generative models is unexplored, especially in the musical domain. This moves beyond using metrics for ranking or filtering outputs to actively shaping the model's probability distribution.

proposals.title: Beyond a Single Reference: Modeling Stylistic Diversity in Human Music and Text Generation
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of text and music generation models, including Music Language Models (MLMs), often assumes a single, static 'human' reference distribution. This assumption is flawed, as human creativity exhibits vast stylistic diversity. Standard metrics like BLEU or FAD may penalize valid stylistic variations, conflating them with errors, thus misrepresenting model quality and limiting our understanding of generative capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: Human-generated text and music are better modeled by a mixture of stylistic distributions rather than a single reference. Explicitly modeling this diversity will lead to more nuanced and accurate evaluation metrics for generative models.
proposals.method: We will leverage pre-trained foundation models (e.g., Jukebox, MusicLM, or large language models) as feature extractors to create a latent space of stylistic attributes. Using clustering techniques (e.g., Gaussian Mixture Models or hierarchical clustering) on large, diverse datasets of human-written lyrics and symbolic music (e.g., Hookpad data, Lakh MIDI), we will identify distinct stylistic clusters. We will then develop new evaluation metrics that compare a generated sample's likelihood under this mixture of stylistic distributions, rather than a single average reference.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a formal framework for quantifying stylistic diversity in human creative output. It will yield new, more robust evaluation metrics for MLMs and text generators that reward stylistic coherence without penalizing legitimate variation. The resulting clusters and analysis will offer interpretable insights into the dimensions of musical and lyrical style.
proposals.novelty: While style transfer and controlled generation exist, explicitly modeling the *reference distribution* for evaluation as a mixture model is a novel perspective. This directly challenges a core, often implicit, assumption in NLP and MIR evaluation, moving beyond average-case metrics to a pluralistic view of quality.

proposals.title: Evaluating Embedding Sensitivity for MAUVE in Music Language Model Assessment
proposals.motivation: MAUVE is a leading metric for evaluating the quality and diversity of text generated by language models, but its reliance on a single, general-domain RoBERTa embedding space may limit its effectiveness for specialized domains like music. As Music Language Models (MLMs) become more sophisticated, robust and domain-sensitive evaluation is critical for meaningful progress. This work investigates whether the choice of embedding space significantly impacts MAUVE's reliability for assessing musical text.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that using domain-specific or multimodal sentence embeddings, such as those from music-audio-text models, will yield MAUVE scores that better correlate with human judgments of musical coherence and quality than the standard RoBERTa-based implementation.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MAUVE algorithm to compute divergences between generated and reference text distributions in alternative embedding spaces. We will test embeddings from general models (e.g., Sentence-BERT), domain-tuned language models (e.g., models fine-tuned on music theory corpora), and multimodal audio-text encoders (e.g., CLAP). The evaluation will be conducted on outputs from existing MLMs using the datasets and human evaluation benchmarks established in Thickstun's prior work on factual and musical evaluation metrics.
proposals.contribution: This study will provide concrete guidance on configuring MAUVE for music generation tasks, potentially leading to a more valid automatic evaluation protocol. It will also contribute a deeper understanding of how semantic embedding choice affects distributional comparison metrics, a question underexplored in the original MAUVE paper.
proposals.novelty: While MAUVE's sensitivity to model parameters has been noted, a systematic study of embedding space alternatives, particularly for a non-standard linguistic domain like music, has not been conducted. This directly connects advanced NLP evaluation techniques to the specific challenges of music informatics.

proposals.title: MAUVE-Guided Reinforcement Learning for Coherent Music Language Model Generation
proposals.motivation: Current Music Language Models (MLMs) trained via maximum likelihood often produce generic or incoherent musical sequences, lacking the long-range structure and stylistic consistency of human compositions. Existing evaluation metrics like those in Thickstun's prior work are post-hoc and do not directly guide the training process. Integrating a distributional similarity metric like MAUVE into the training loop could directly optimize for the quality and diversity of generated music.
proposals.hypothesis: Using MAUVE as a reward signal for reinforcement learning (RL) will enable the direct optimization of a Music Language Model's output distribution, leading to generated symbolic music that is more stylistically coherent and structurally similar to a target human corpus than models trained solely with teacher forcing.
proposals.method: We will fine-tune a pre-trained transformer-based Music Language Model (e.g., a variant of MuseNet or a model from the Music Transformer family) using Proximal Policy Optimization (PPO). The reward function will be computed using the MAUVE algorithm, which measures the divergence between the n-gram distributions of the model's generated sequences and a reference dataset of high-quality symbolic music (e.g., Lakh MIDI). We will implement a batched, efficient version of MAUVE suitable for RL loops and experiment with reward shaping, including combining MAUVE with a factual consistency reward based on chord/key grounding.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel training paradigm for MLMs that directly optimizes for distributional quality, moving beyond next-token prediction. It will provide empirical results on whether distribution-matching objectives improve perceived musicality and coherence in generated scores. The resulting model and training framework will be a significant step towards controllable, high-fidelity AI music generation.
proposals.novelty: While MAUVE has been used for evaluation, its application as a differentiable training signal for RL in the domain of symbolic music generation is novel. This approach bypasses the need for hand-crafted reward functions and directly aligns the model's output distribution with a desired corpus.

proposals.title: Inverse MAUVE Optimization: Training Text-to-Music Models via Divergence Minimization
proposals.motivation: MAUVE is a leading metric for evaluating the quality and diversity of generated text, but it is used post-hoc as a static evaluation. In music generation, where models like Hookpad Aria produce symbolic music as a form of structured text, there is a critical need for training objectives that directly optimize for high-quality, human-like distributions. This project addresses the inverse problem: can we directly train a generative model by minimizing its MAUVE score against a reference corpus?
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a text-to-music model can be trained using a differentiable approximation of the MAUVE score as a loss function, leading to generated musical sequences (e.g., in ABC notation or a tokenized piano roll) that are more qualitatively aligned with human compositions than models trained with standard maximum likelihood.
proposals.method: We will adapt the MAUVE computation, which compares quantized divergence curves of GPT-2 embeddings, into a differentiable loss. Using a pre-trained embedding model (e.g., from the SENSE family), we will compute soft histograms over embedding clusters for both generated and reference batches. We will then train a transformer-based music language model (initialized from a model like Jukebox's prior or a MuseNet architecture) using gradient descent to minimize the KL divergences that underpin the MAUVE curve, employing a REINFORCE-style gradient estimator or a Gumbel-Softmax relaxation for discrete token sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel training paradigm for sequence generation that directly optimizes a state-of-the-art distributional similarity metric, 2) a new, potentially higher-quality text-conditioned music generation model, and 3) an analysis of whether optimizing for MAUVE improves human evaluations of musicality and structure over baseline models.
proposals.novelty: While MAUVE is widely used for evaluation, using it as a direct, differentiable training objective for discrete sequence generation is unexplored. Applying this inverse approach specifically to symbolic music generation, where the 'text' has rich hierarchical structure, is a novel synthesis of metric design and model training.

proposals.title: Divergence Frontiers for Music Language Models: Quantifying the Gap Between Human and Machine-Generated Musical Text
proposals.motivation: As Music Language Models (MLMs) become more capable of generating symbolic music (e.g., ABC notation, MIDI-like tokens), there is a critical need for rigorous, quantitative evaluation beyond surface-level metrics. Pre-2026 work, including Thickstun's own on factual and musical evaluation, highlights that models can produce plausible outputs that lack the nuanced structure and creativity of human composition. A principled statistical framework is needed to measure not just quality, but the fundamental distributional differences between human and machine-generated musical text.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the distribution of music generated by state-of-the-art MLMs occupies a distinct, narrower manifold than human compositions, characterized by systematic over-representation of common patterns (lack of originality) and under-coverage of rare but musically valid structures (lack of diversity).
proposals.method: We will adapt the Divergence Frontier (DF) framework, a pre-2026 statistical tool for comparing distributions, to the domain of symbolic music. Using a large corpus of human music (e.g., Hookpad user data or public symbolic datasets) as a reference distribution P, and samples from MLMs (e.g., models fine-tuned from MuseNet or similar architectures) as Q, we will compute the trade-off between Type I error (rejecting a human sample) and Type II error (accepting a machine sample). The test statistic will be based on a robust neural audio fingerprinting model (adapted from Thickstun's prior work) to embed musical sequences into a semantic feature space where the divergence is calculated.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first application of divergence frontiers to music generation, yielding a interpretable, two-dimensional metric for model assessment. It will produce a concrete, scalable methodology for diagnosing specific failure modes (e.g., over-reliance on common chord progressions) in MLMs. The resulting analysis will inform future model development by pinpointing the precise aspects of musical creativity that are hardest for machines to capture.
proposals.novelty: While divergence frontiers have been explored in natural language, their application to structured, sequential musical text is novel. Combining this statistical framework with a music-specific embedding model (derived from audio fingerprinting) creates a unique evaluation tool grounded in musical semantics rather than just token likelihood.

proposals.title: Predicting Interactive Music Generation Quality: Automated Proxies for Subjective Human-in-the-Loop Metrics
proposals.motivation: Evaluating interactive music generation systems, like AI songwriting copilots (e.g., Hookpad Aria), relies heavily on costly and slow human-in-the-loop studies to measure subjective qualities like creativity, coherence, and user satisfaction. This bottleneck severely hinders the rapid iteration and development of such systems. An automated, reliable proxy for these subjective metrics would dramatically accelerate research and deployment in human-AI music co-creation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a multimodal model, trained on a dataset of human interaction traces and ratings, can learn to predict subjective interactive evaluation metrics directly from system outputs and interaction context, achieving correlation with human judgments that is sufficient for rapid prototyping and model selection.
proposals.method: We will collect a dataset of interaction logs and post-session ratings from users of Hookpad Aria. Using this data, we will train a neural network that takes as input the generated musical material (encoded via a pre-trained music foundation model like Jukebox or a symbolic encoder), the user's preceding context, and the interaction history. The model will be trained via multi-task learning to predict the suite of subjective ratings. We will experiment with architectures combining transformer encoders for sequence modeling and cross-attention layers to fuse musical and textual interaction contexts.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first validated framework for automated, predictive evaluation of interactive music generation systems, reducing dependency on human studies. It will contribute a public dataset of interaction traces with human ratings and a set of trained proxy models that can be used as benchmarks for future research in music AI and human-computer co-creation.
proposals.novelty: While automated metrics exist for offline music generation, predicting subjective interactive quality from user-AI dialogue and musical context is a novel challenge. This moves beyond static output evaluation to model the dynamic, co-creative process central to tools like Aria.

proposals.title: Evaluating Co-Creation: Interactive Metrics for Human-LM Music Generation
proposals.motivation: Current evaluation of Music Language Models (MLMs) focuses on static outputs, using metrics like factual accuracy or musicality. However, models like Hookpad Aria are designed for interactive, iterative collaboration with human songwriters. This gap means we lack the tools to measure what makes an MLM a good creative partner—its ability to inspire, adapt, and maintain coherence throughout a dynamic creative process.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that interactive metrics, which capture the temporal dynamics and subjective quality of a human-LM co-creation session, will provide a more meaningful and predictive evaluation of a model's utility in real-world creative tasks than static output-based metrics alone.
proposals.method: We will design and validate a suite of interactive metrics. First, we will instrument Hookpad Aria to log detailed interaction traces (e.g., user edits, model suggestions accepted/rejected, session duration). Second, we will develop algorithms to compute metrics such as: (1) *Adaptation Coherence* (measuring thematic consistency of model suggestions across turns using embeddings from music foundation models like Jukebox or MusicLM), (2) *Creative Lead* (quantifying how often a model suggestion initiates a new productive branch in the session graph), and (3) *Iteration Efficiency*. Third, we will conduct controlled user studies where songwriters complete tasks with different MLM backends, collecting subjective ratings on collaboration quality to correlate with our automated metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first formal framework for evaluating MLMs as interactive systems, moving beyond single-output assessment. It will deliver a publicly available benchmark suite and validated metrics that can guide the development of more useful, human-centered AI creative tools. The methodology will also be generalizable to other domains of human-LM collaboration.
proposals.novelty: While prior work (including Thickstun's) has created metrics for musical output, no existing work defines metrics for the *process* of human-AI co-creation in music. This shifts the evaluation paradigm from judging a final artifact to assessing the collaborative journey.

proposals.title: AnchorSetNet: A Unified Bidirectional Transformer for Music and Text Generation via Anchor Set Completion
proposals.motivation: Current generative models for music and text are often specialized for specific tasks like prefix continuation or infilling, requiring separate architectures or training objectives. This fragmentation limits model flexibility and complicates the development of integrated creative tools, such as AI songwriting assistants that need to handle both melodic continuation and harmonic infilling seamlessly. A unified model capable of robustly generating from arbitrary, sparse constraints would represent a significant step towards more general and controllable sequence generation.
proposals.hypothesis: A non-causal transformer, trained exclusively with a consistent masked generation loss on all non-anchor positions given a sparse, unordered set of 'anchor' tokens, can develop planning and integration capabilities that allow it to match or exceed the performance of task-specialized models across the full spectrum of generation tasks, from prefix completion to infilling.
proposals.method: We will implement a non-causal transformer encoder architecture, inspired by BERT-style masked language modeling but adapted for generation. The model will be trained on symbolic music data (e.g., REMI or structured MIDI) and text corpora. For each training sequence, a random subset of tokens is designated as the immutable 'anchor set'; the model's objective is to reconstruct the entire original sequence by predicting all non-anchor tokens in a single bidirectional pass. We will evaluate against specialized baselines (e.g., causal LMs for continuation, models like Insertion Transformer for infilling) using the factual and musical evaluation metrics established in Thickstun's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete, measurable test of unified sequence generation, potentially delivering a single model that performs robustly across multiple generation modalities. It will contribute a novel training paradigm and architecture for controllable generation in creative domains, directly applicable to next-generation AI music co-creation tools like an advanced Hookpad Aria.
proposals.novelty: While masked modeling is well-established, its exclusive application to a consistent 'anchor set completion' task for full-sequence generation, explicitly unifying prefix and infilling tasks under a single objective, is a novel formulation. This approach moves beyond prior infilling methods that often rely on iterative or specialized masking schemes.

proposals.title: Harmonizing Structure and Nuance: A Consistency-Driven Dual-Stream Model for Music Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models face a trade-off: symbolic models capture high-level structure but lack expressive nuance, while audio models capture timbral detail but often lack musical coherence. This gap limits the creation of AI tools that are both musically intelligent and sonically rich, a core challenge for applications like Hookpad Aria. A unified representation is needed to bridge symbolic abstraction and audio realism.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that jointly learning a discrete, grammar-aware symbolic stream and a continuous, performance-nuance stream, regularized by a novel structural consistency objective, will yield a hybrid representation that supports generation of music that is both structurally coherent and expressively detailed.
proposals.method: We will build a dual-encoder architecture. The discrete stream will use a tokenizer (inspired by MuLaN or Jukebox's lyric/artist tokens) trained on symbolic features (e.g., chords, beats, note onsets) extracted via pre-2026 MIR tools. The continuous stream will use a contrastive audio encoder (like CLAP or a modified Jukebox encoder). The core innovation is an adversarial consistency module that forces the continuous embeddings to be predictable from the discrete token sequence, preventing the continuous stream from collapsing into purely acoustic features unrelated to musical structure. Training will use a combination of reconstruction (for audio/symbols), contrastive (for audio-text alignment), and the proposed consistency loss.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel hybrid music representation that explicitly couples structure and nuance, 2) a new adversarial consistency training objective for multi-modal learning, and 3) a foundation for more coherent and expressive music generation models, advancing the evaluation paradigms explored in Thickstun's prior work on musical metrics.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has combined symbolic and audio representations (e.g., MusicLM), our method's novelty lies in the explicit, adversarial consistency constraint that grounds continuous audio features in discrete musical grammar, ensuring the hybrid representation maintains structural semantics rather than treating the two streams independently.

proposals.title: Structured Music Language Modeling: Integrating Hierarchical Form and Long-Term Coherence
proposals.motivation: Current music language models (MLMs) and generation systems, including those based on symbolic MIDI or tokenized audio, often struggle to capture high-level musical structure (e.g., verse-chorus form, thematic development) and long-range dependencies. This results in generated pieces that lack the coherent, large-scale architecture characteristic of compelling human compositions, limiting their practical utility for songwriting and composition assistance.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that explicitly modeling hierarchical musical structure within a transformer-based MLM, using a multi-scale representation and a structured attention mechanism, will significantly improve the long-term coherence and formal integrity of generated musical sequences.
proposals.method: We will extend the SENSE/ARIA framework by developing a multi-level tokenization scheme that explicitly marks structural boundaries (e.g., section labels, phrase endings). We will then train a modified transformer architecture incorporating a form-aware attention bias, such as a block-sparse or hierarchical attention mask, to enforce structural priors. This model will be trained and evaluated on the Lakh MIDI dataset and proprietary Hookpad data, using the factual and musical evaluation metrics from prior work to assess improvements in long-range coherence.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete method for integrating structural musical knowledge into neural generation models, moving beyond local note-by-note prediction. It will deliver a new, open-source MLM capable of generating musically coherent multi-section pieces and contribute a set of evaluation protocols for assessing long-term structure in generated music.
proposals.novelty: While prior work has explored hierarchical representations for music, the integration of explicit, learnable structural constraints into the attention mechanism of a large-scale MLM, specifically for the task of coherent long-form generation, remains underexplored. This approach moves beyond simple positional encoding to model the functional relationships between musical sections.

proposals.title: Hierarchical Musical Planning for Coherent Long-Form Audio Generation
proposals.motivation: Current music language models (MLMs) and audio generation systems, including autoregressive models, excel at local coherence but struggle with maintaining consistent, high-level musical structure over long time horizons (e.g., minutes). This leads to meandering compositions lacking clear form, development, or satisfying long-term resolution, limiting their utility for professional songwriting and composition. The problem is analogous to long-horizon planning challenges in sequential decision-making.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating a hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) planner, operating on abstract musical representations, into an autoregressive audio generation model will enable the anticipatory generation of more coherent and structurally satisfying long-form musical pieces.
proposals.method: We will adapt the HRL framework of Options or HIRO to music. A high-level planner will operate on a latent space derived from the SENSE or ARIA model, setting multi-bar goals (e.g., 'build tension,' 'transition to chorus'). A low-level policy, the existing autoregressive audio generator, will be trained with an intrinsic reward to achieve these subgoals. The planner will be trained using a model-based or offline RL approach on a dataset of structured songs (e.g., Hookpad data), learning a policy over abstract musical actions to maximize a reward based on music-theoretic and learned structural metrics.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute 1) a novel HRL architecture for long-horizon audio generation, 2) a new benchmark and evaluation protocol for long-term musical coherence, and 3) a practical system that demonstrates improved structural planning, moving beyond local token prediction to guided musical narrative.
proposals.novelty: While HRL is established in robotics and game playing, its application to direct raw audio generation for artistic structure is novel. This approach fundamentally shifts the generation paradigm from next-token prediction to goal-conditioned, temporally abstract planning within the musical domain.

proposals.title: Synthesizing Musicality: A Programmatic Generation Framework with Learned Aesthetic Critics
proposals.motivation: Current music generation systems face a trade-off between explicit control and musical quality. Pure neural models (e.g., MusicLM) produce fluid audio but are opaque and hard to steer, while programmatic or symbolic systems offer structure but often sound rigid and lack expressive nuance. Bridging this gap is essential for creating AI songwriting assistants, like Hookpad Aria, that are both intuitive for users and capable of producing professionally compelling material.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid system that uses program synthesis to generate symbolic music, guided by a neural aesthetic critic trained on human judgments of musicality, will produce outputs that surpass purely programmatic systems in perceived quality while retaining superior controllability compared to end-to-end neural generators.
proposals.method: We will build a synthesis engine that generates programs in a domain-specific language (DSL) for music, inspired by prior work on program synthesis for audio. Candidate programs are executed to produce MIDI sequences. A pre-trained music foundation model (e.g., Jukebox, or a model adapted from the Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting work) will be fine-tuned as a critic on a dataset of human quality ratings (extending the evaluation methodology from 'Factual and Musical Evaluation Metrics for Music Language Models'). This critic's scores will guide a Monte Carlo Tree Search or evolutionary algorithm to iteratively refine the program towards higher aesthetic scores, optimizing for qualities like natural phrasing and dynamic expression.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, controllable music generation system and a framework for integrating explicit symbolic reasoning with learned aesthetic models. It will also contribute a new benchmark for evaluating the musicality of structured generative systems, advancing the field towards more steerable and artistically viable AI tools.
proposals.novelty: While program synthesis and neural critics exist separately, their tight integration for *musical* program generation is unexplored. The key novelty is using a pre-trained music foundation model as a differentiable guide within a discrete program search, creating a direct feedback loop between structure and learned aesthetic preference.

proposals.title: Programmatic Music Generation via Neural Program Synthesis and a Domain-Specific Language for Structure
proposals.motivation: Current music language models (MLMs) generate sequences of tokens (e.g., MIDI events) but offer limited high-level control over musical form and long-range structure, making them difficult for creators to steer. Program synthesis, which generates executable code from specifications, offers a promising paradigm for creating structured, editable, and controllable musical outputs, bridging the gap between high-level compositional intent and low-level audio or symbolic generation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a neural program synthesis model, trained to generate programs in a domain-specific language (DSL) for music, can produce more structurally coherent and user-controllable compositions than end-to-end sequence models, while maintaining high musical quality as evaluated by both automated metrics and human listeners.
proposals.method: We will design a music-specific DSL that encodes musical elements (e.g., motifs, chord progressions, sections, repetitions, variations) as composable functions. We will then train a transformer-based program synthesis model, using techniques from prior work like Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting and Hookpad Aria for data representation, to generate DSL programs from natural language prompts or musical sketches. The model will be trained on a corpus of symbolic music (e.g., MIDI) paired with automatically inferred or human-annotated programmatic representations. Execution of the generated program will yield a structured symbolic score, which can be rendered to audio.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute: 1) a novel program synthesis framework for controllable music generation, 2) a new, publicly available DSL for representing musical structure, and 3) an evaluation benchmark assessing both the structural fidelity and musical quality of programmatically generated music, extending the factual and musical evaluation metrics from Thickstun's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While program synthesis has been applied to image generation and robotics, its application to structured music generation is nascent. This approach uniquely combines the high-level control of symbolic AI with the generative capacity of neural networks, moving beyond sequence prediction to the generation of interpretable, editable musical 'code'.

proposals.title: Orchestral ScoreNet: Anticipatory Modeling of Polyphonic Multi-Track Music with Hierarchical Transformers
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models, including Music Language Models (MLLMs), are primarily trained on symbolic datasets like MIDI with limited polyphony or on audio with limited track separation, failing to capture the complex, anticipatory interplay of a full orchestra. The creation of rich, multi-track orchestral music remains a significant challenge for AI, requiring models that understand long-term dependencies and intricate voice-leading across dozens of simultaneous instrumental lines. Scaling to massive, high-fidelity multi-track datasets is the necessary next step to move beyond melody-and-chord generation towards genuine orchestral composition.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a hierarchical transformer architecture, trained on a large-scale dataset of aligned multi-track symbolic scores, can learn to anticipate and generate coherent, polyphonic orchestral music by modeling long-range dependencies both within individual instrumental tracks and across the entire ensemble.
proposals.method: We will construct a large dataset by extracting and aligning individual instrumental parts from existing symbolic score libraries (e.g., MuseScore, Lakh MIDI). The model will be a two-level encoder-decoder transformer. The lower-level encoder processes each instrumental track separately using a modified version of the SENSE or Lead architecture for temporal modeling. A higher-level cross-attention mechanism, inspired by work on multi-modal fusion, will integrate information across all tracks to learn orchestral dependencies. Training will use a masked prediction objective (like Hookpad's anticipatory training) to predict future note events across the ensemble, conditioned on the full multi-track history.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first large-scale neural model capable of generating and completing multi-track orchestral scores with complex polyphony. It will provide new, rigorous evaluation metrics for polyphonic and multi-track music generation, extending the researcher's prior work on factual and musical evaluation. The resulting model and dataset will serve as a foundational tool for computational musicology and assistive composition.
proposals.novelty: While prior work on MLLMs and generation (e.g., MuseNet, Music Transformer) handles polyphony, scaling to the explicit, separate modeling of 20+ simultaneous orchestral tracks with a hierarchical anticipatory objective is novel. This moves beyond generating a monolithic piano roll to modeling the distinct roles and interactions of a symphony orchestra's sections.

proposals.title: EdgeSense: On-Device Continual Learning for Real-Time Music Anticipation
proposals.motivation: Edge-based music applications, such as intelligent audio effects, interactive composition tools, and responsive media players, require models that can anticipate musical structure in real-time. Current approaches rely on static, cloud-trained models that cannot adapt to individual user styles or evolving acoustic environments, leading to poor personalization and performance drift. A solution that enables efficient, private, and continual on-device learning is critical for next-generation interactive music systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A lightweight neural predictor, combined with an efficient on-device rehearsal buffer and elastic weight consolidation, can enable effective continual learning for music anticipation, mitigating catastrophic forgetting while adapting to user-specific patterns under strict hardware constraints.
proposals.method: We will develop a compact transformer or LSTM-based core predictor, inspired by the efficient architectures in Thickstun's 'sense' and 'lead' work, to forecast upcoming audio features. For continual adaptation, we will implement a fixed-size ring buffer for replay of past user data and integrate Elastic Weight Consolidation (EWC) to penalize changes to weights important for previous tasks. The system will be trained and evaluated on mobile-class hardware using datasets of user interaction sequences (e.g., from Hookpad Aria) and diverse musical audio.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel framework for continual, on-device learning in music AI, directly addressing the problem of catastrophic forgetting in resource-constrained settings. It will provide new evaluation protocols, extending Thickstun's work on factual and musical metrics to the continual learning domain, and a deployable model for personalized, privacy-preserving edge music applications.
proposals.novelty: While continual learning is studied in computer vision, its application to real-time music anticipation on edge devices is unexplored. The integration of efficient rehearsal with EWC specifically for temporal audio models, operating entirely on-device, presents a significant advance over static cloud models.

proposals.title: Inverse Control Attribution: Recovering Latent Guidance from Non-Autoregressive Music and Text Generations
proposals.motivation: Non-autoregressive generative models (e.g., diffusion models, masked token models) are increasingly used for music and text synthesis due to their parallelism and high-quality outputs. However, their one-shot generation process obscures the latent control parameters (e.g., prompts, style vectors, constraints) that guided the creation, complicating authorship attribution and raising concerns about undisclosed conditioning or AI-generated content. A method to infer this original generative intent is needed for transparency and provenance.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a recoverable, distortion-free signature within the control process of a non-autoregressive model, we can reliably invert the generation to infer the original latent guidance parameters, even in the absence of sequential token dependencies.
proposals.method: We will adapt principles from pre-2026 distortion-free watermarking and steganography (e.g., techniques for embedding signals in latent spaces) to the control mechanism of a diffusion-based music generation model. The method will involve a two-stage training of a conditional generator: first, to produce high-quality outputs given control vectors; second, to embed a structured, recoverable signature within the model's internal sampling trajectory. A lightweight inverse network will then be trained to extract this signature and reconstruct the original control vector from a generated piece. Evaluation will use the researcher's existing musical evaluation metrics and factual consistency checks on text.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first framework for verifiable attribution and intent recovery in non-autoregressive generative models for music and language. It will yield a practical tool for detecting undisclosed conditioning (e.g., a hidden prompt) and establishing a chain of provenance for AI-generated creative works, advancing accountability in generative AI.
proposals.novelty: The approach solves the inverse problem for one-shot generation models where traditional autoregressive tracing is impossible, and does so by embedding a signature in the control process itself, not in the output tokens, ensuring minimal quality degradation.

proposals.title: Inverse Music Control: Extracting Latent Guidance from Audio with Diffusion Posterior Sampling
proposals.motivation: Modern music generation models like SENSE and Hookpad Aria use latent control processes (e.g., chords, structure, melody) to guide audio synthesis, but these controls are lost after rendering. Recovering them from raw audio is a critical inverse problem for music understanding, editing, and remixing. Solving it would enable powerful applications like intelligent stems separation, style transfer, and automated music analysis, bridging the gap between generative models and practical music production tools.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the latent control process used by a conditional diffusion model for music generation can be accurately inferred from the generated audio alone by leveraging the model's own internal representations through diffusion posterior sampling and a learned inference network.
proposals.method: We will build upon the SENSE architecture, a latent diffusion model for music. The core technical approach involves training a control inference network that operates in the model's latent space. This network will be trained jointly with the diffusion model using a variational objective, learning to map from a latent audio representation to the distribution of possible conditioning controls (e.g., chord, melody, structure tags). For inference on arbitrary audio, we will first encode it into the latent space using a fine-tuned encoder, then apply the trained inference network, refining the estimate via a few steps of diffusion posterior sampling to ensure consistency with the audio.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first general-purpose framework for extracting high-level, editable controls from arbitrary music audio, directly linked to a state-of-the-art generative model's vocabulary. It will contribute a new benchmark and evaluation protocol for the inverse music control task, extending the factual evaluation metrics from Thickstun's prior work. The resulting tool will be integrated into a prototype for Hookpad Aria, demonstrating practical songwriting applications.
proposals.novelty: While prior work extracts low-level features or symbolic representations, this method uniquely targets the recovery of the specific, high-level latent controls used by a modern generative model, framing it as an inverse diffusion problem. This approach is non-obvious as it leverages the generative model's own architecture for inference, rather than treating it as a black-box analysis task.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Stylistic Adaptation of Music Language Models via Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models require vast datasets for training, limiting their ability to capture the nuanced style of a specific composer or artist without extensive, curated data. This is a critical barrier for practical songwriting tools like Hookpad Aria, which aim to offer personalized, style-specific assistance. Enabling adaptation with just a few examples would unlock new applications in co-creative systems and musicological analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) methods, such as Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) applied to a pre-trained anticipatory music model, can effectively capture a specific composer's stylistic signature using only a few musical examples, without catastrophic forgetting of general musical knowledge.
proposals.method: We will build upon a pre-trained music language model (e.g., a Transformer decoder trained on symbolic music). For adaptation, we will apply Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) modules to the model's attention layers, freezing the original weights and training only the new low-rank matrices. We will evaluate this approach using the 'sense' and 'lead' anticipatory modeling framework on datasets of single-composer works (e.g., Bach chorales, Beatles songs), comparing few-shot LoRA fine-tuning to full fine-tuning and a pre-trained baseline. Evaluation will use the factual and musical metrics from prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a concrete methodology for data-efficient personalization of music AI, directly applicable to next-generation co-creative tools. It will also yield new insights into the representational capacity of music foundation models, showing which stylistic features are captured in low-rank adaptations.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT is established in NLP, its application to music generation for few-shot stylistic adaptation is novel. The work uniquely combines the anticipatory modeling paradigm ('sense', 'lead') with efficient adaptation to model composer-specific 'time' and 'hook' structures.

proposals.title: Disentangled Adversarial Panels for Robust Multi-Aspect Optimization of Music Language Models
proposals.motivation: Current Music Language Models (MLMs) are often evaluated and optimized using single, monolithic metrics or discriminators, which can lead to models that 'game' a specific objective at the expense of other crucial qualities like factual accuracy, structural coherence, or stylistic authenticity. This results in brittle and unreliable generation systems. A method is needed to enforce balanced improvement across multiple, distinct quality dimensions simultaneously, a problem directly relevant to the researcher's recent work on factual and musical evaluation metrics.
proposals.hypothesis: Replacing a single adversarial discriminator with a panel of disentangled, specialized critics, whose influence is dynamically weighted by a meta-controller, will force a generator to improve holistically across defined quality aspects, leading to more robust and controllable music generation.
