proposals.method: We will design a modular adversarial framework for MLMs. First, we will train separate critic networks, each on a distinct, pre-2026 evaluation task: one for factual grounding (e.g., lyric consistency), one for instruction following (using datasets like Hookpad Aria's prompts), and one for stylistic/musical integrity (using embeddings from a pre-trained audio foundation model like the one from the Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting paper). A lightweight meta-controller network, trained via reinforcement learning, will observe the generator's outputs and dynamically adjust the weight of each critic's loss contribution to the generator's total adversarial loss, preventing over-optimization on any single dimension.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel training paradigm for controllable and robust generative models in music. It will yield a diagnostic framework to identify specific failure modes in MLMs and a flexible architecture where new quality critics can be added incrementally. The resulting model should demonstrate superior multi-aspect performance on established benchmarks.
proposals.novelty: While multi-task learning and ensemble discriminators exist, the integration of a real-time, learnable meta-controller to dynamically reweight a panel of disentangled adversarial critics for anticipatory generation in music is a novel approach to preventing metric gaming and ensuring balanced model improvement.

proposals.title: Rationale-Conditioned Text Generation for Interpretable Music and Language Modeling
proposals.motivation: Current text generation models, including music language models, produce outputs from prompts but lack explicit, human-interpretable control over the internal reasoning that leads to those outputs. This limits their utility as collaborative tools, as in songwriting co-pilots, where understanding the 'why' behind a generated lyric or chord progression is crucial. Investigating the inverse problem—generating a full text (e.g., a song section or descriptive paragraph) from a task label and its rationale—can lead to more interpretable, steerable, and trustworthy generative systems.
proposals.hypothesis: A sequence-to-sequence model, explicitly conditioned on a structured rationale, can generate coherent and contextually appropriate text inputs (musical or linguistic) that faithfully reflect the provided reasoning, and this process will improve the interpretability and user control of generative models.
proposals.method: We will adapt a Transformer-based encoder-decoder architecture. The input will be a concatenated sequence of a task label (e.g., 'write a chorus') and a structured rationale (e.g., 'evoke nostalgia, use a IV-V-I progression, include a melodic hook'). The model will be trained on datasets where text outputs are paired with human-annotated or synthetically generated rationales, using a standard autoregressive language modeling objective. For musical text (e.g., chord sequences, lead sheets), we will leverage tokenization methods from Hookpad Aria and related music foundation models. Evaluation will use the factual and musical metrics from Thickstun's prior work, augmented with rationale faithfulness scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel framework for rationale-conditioned generation, directly applicable to co-pilot tools for creative domains like songwriting. It will yield a new benchmark task and evaluation suite for assessing the interpretability and controllability of generative models, moving beyond output quality to the transparency of the generative process.
proposals.novelty: While prior work uses rationales to explain model outputs, we invert this pipeline to use rationales as a conditioning signal for generation, offering fine-grained, interpretable control. Applying this specifically to structured musical text generation, building on pre-2026 music foundation models, is an unexplored direction.

proposals.title: LatentSync: Imperceptible Watermarking for Non-Autoregressive Audio Generation via Learned Continuous Representations
proposals.motivation: The proliferation of high-fidelity, non-autoregressive generative models for audio and music, such as diffusion and flow models, creates a critical need for provenance tracking to combat misuse and ensure ethical deployment. Existing watermarking techniques are designed for autoregressive models with discrete token spaces and are incompatible with the parallel generation and continuous latent spaces of these newer architectures. Without a solution, the field lacks a reliable mechanism to attribute AI-generated audio content, hindering responsible adoption.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a statistical watermark can be embedded directly within the learned continuous representation of a non-autoregressive model by applying a perceptually-aligned perturbation during the generation process, and that this signal can be reliably detected without degrading audio quality or generation speed.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where a lightweight watermarking module is integrated into the latent space of a pre-trained diffusion or flow-based audio model (e.g., leveraging the continuous latent structure of models like Stable Audio). The module will apply a learned, low-norm perturbation vector to the latent trajectory, optimized via a multi-objective loss that minimizes perceptual distortion (using a pre-trained audio fingerprinting model like the one from 'Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting') while maximizing detectability by a paired detector. The detector will be trained to recognize the statistical signature of the perturbation in the generated audio's latent features.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first dedicated watermarking framework for non-autoregressive audio generation models, enabling reliable provenance for AI-generated music and sound. It will contribute a novel evaluation protocol for watermark imperceptibility in audio, extending beyond simple signal metrics to include musical and perceptual assessments inspired by 'Factual and Musical Evaluation Metrics for Music Language Models'.
proposals.novelty: This approach is novel in moving watermarking from discrete, sequential token spaces to continuous, parallel latent spaces, and in leveraging the inherent structure of a pre-trained generative model's representation to hide a signal, rather than operating on the output waveform directly.

proposals.title: Learning Audio Representations with Weak Multi-Modal Supervision for Music Generation
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models often rely on large datasets of audio paired with precise note-level transcriptions (e.g., MIDI), which are expensive and time-consuming to create, limiting dataset scale and diversity. This reliance on strong supervision constrains models to a narrow, symbolic view of music, potentially hindering their ability to learn richer, more expressive audio representations directly from the signal. A method that can learn from abundant, weakly-aligned data (like audio paired with text descriptions or chord charts) would unlock larger, more varied datasets and more flexible models.
proposals.hypothesis: A music foundation model can learn robust, temporally-structured audio representations using only weak or multi-modal supervision (e.g., song-level tags, lyrical phrases, or chord sequence labels), and these representations will be sufficient for high-quality conditional music generation tasks without precise note-level labels.
proposals.method: We will extend the SENSE (Self-Supervised Note Embeddings) or ARIA architecture to accept multiple, weakly-aligned conditioning streams. The model will be trained on a dataset of audio paired with text descriptions, hook-level structural labels (inspired by Hookpad data), and coarse harmonic annotations. A contrastive or masked prediction objective will align these modalities within a shared latent space. For generation, we will adapt the time-controlled latent diffusion approach from recent work to be conditioned on these weak labels, using a classifier-free guidance mechanism to ensure fidelity to the multi-modal input.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new framework for training music audio models without expensive note-level supervision, democratizing the development of high-quality generative systems. It will also deliver a pre-trained model capable of conditional generation (e.g., "a chorus with a driving bassline") from flexible, high-level descriptions, evaluated using the factual and musical metrics established in Thickstun's prior work.
proposals.novelty: While self-supervised audio representation learning exists, applying it specifically for conditional music generation using multiple streams of weak, semantically rich supervision (text, structure, harmony) is unexplored. This challenges the prevailing assumption that high-fidelity music generation requires strongly supervised symbolic pre-training.

proposals.title: Structured Audio Generation via Hierarchical Latent Tokenization
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models, including autoregressive Music Language Models (MLMs), often operate at the note or frame level, leading to outputs that can lack coherent musical structure and long-term consistency. This note-by-note focus mirrors early text generation before the adoption of subword tokenization, creating a bottleneck for capturing higher-level musical semantics like chord progressions and phrase contours, which are critical for listener engagement and compositional quality.
proposals.hypothesis: By designing a hierarchical audio tokenizer that explicitly represents chords and rhythmic phrases as discrete latent units, we can train a transformer-based MLM to generate more musically structured and coherent audio sequences directly, improving both objective metrics of structure and subjective listener ratings.
proposals.method: We will extend the Jukebox/VQ-VAE framework to learn a two-tier discrete latent representation: a fine-level code for timbre and pitch nuance, and a coarse-level codebook where tokens correspond to predefined chord qualities (e.g., Cmaj7) and rhythmic phrase templates. Using a dataset of aligned lead sheets and audio (e.g., Hookpad Lead data), we will train a transformer decoder to autoregressively predict sequences of these coarse structural tokens, which are then expanded into the fine tokens and decoded to audio. Evaluation will leverage the factual and musical metrics from Thickstun's prior work, alongside novel chord and phrase consistency scores.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a new model architecture and training methodology for structured audio generation, moving beyond frame-level prediction. It will also deliver a publicly available hierarchical tokenizer for symbolic-audio alignment and a benchmark for evaluating structural coherence in generated music.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in symbolic music generation models chords, applying a hierarchical discrete latent model to directly generate structured *audio* from chord/phrase tokens is unexplored. This bridges the gap between high-level symbolic control (as in Hookpad Aria) and raw audio generation.

proposals.title: Robust Audio Generation and Analysis via Domain Adaptation of Studio-Trained Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current music foundation models and audio generation systems are predominantly trained on high-quality, clean studio recordings. This creates a significant performance gap when applying these models to real-world audio, such as live concert recordings, user-generated content, or historical archives, which contain noise, reverberation, and mixing artifacts. Bridging this gap is crucial for developing robust AI tools for music analysis, retrieval, and creative assistance in authentic, non-studio environments.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that music foundation models pre-trained on studio recordings can be effectively adapted to noisy, real-world audio domains through targeted fine-tuning and data augmentation, without requiring a full re-training from scratch on massive, curated datasets of real-world audio.
proposals.method: We will leverage a pre-trained audio foundation model, such as Jukebox or a robust embedding model from Thickstun's prior work, as a starting point. Using a dataset of paired studio and live recordings (or studio recordings artificially degraded with realistic noise, crowd sounds, and reverb), we will perform domain-adaptive fine-tuning. Techniques like SpecAugment and adversarial domain confusion losses will be employed to encourage the model to learn noise-invariant representations. The adapted model will be evaluated on tasks from Thickstun's recent papers: robust neural fingerprinting for live audio and the generation of musical elements in Hookpad Aria conditioned on noisy inputs.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a methodology for efficiently adapting large-scale audio models to challenging acoustic domains, expanding their practical utility. It will also provide new benchmarks and evaluation protocols, extending Thickstun's work on factual and musical metrics, for assessing model performance on real-world audio generation and analysis tasks.
proposals.novelty: While domain adaptation is common in vision and speech, its systematic application to large-scale music foundation models for both generation and analysis tasks is underexplored. The novelty lies in adapting models designed for pristine audio to the complex, unstructured domain of live music, a significant step towards in-the-wild music AI.

proposals.title: Instrument-Agnostic Note Transcription via Self-Supervised Audio Representation Learning
proposals.motivation: Current automatic music transcription (AMT) models are typically trained and evaluated on datasets with known, often limited, instrument classes, limiting their robustness to real-world audio containing novel or hybrid timbres. This brittleness hinders applications in music information retrieval and generative modeling where instrument identity is ambiguous or unavailable. Developing transcription models that do not rely on explicit prior knowledge of the instrument addresses a core limitation in creating general-purpose music understanding systems.
proposals.hypothesis: By training a model on a diverse corpus of monophonic audio with strong data augmentation on timbre, a self-supervised audio foundation model can learn instrument-agnostic representations that enable accurate note event prediction without explicit instrument conditioning or identification.
proposals.method: We will leverage a large-scale, diverse dataset of monophonic instrumental performances (e.g., URMP, MusicNet, and synthesized data) and apply aggressive audio transformations (pitch shifting, formant shifting, filtering, and neural style transfer) to decouple pitch and timing information from specific timbral signatures. The core architecture will be a Transformer encoder-decoder, similar to the Jukebox autoencoder or a modified Wav2Vec 2.0, pre-trained via a masked acoustic token modeling objective. This pre-trained encoder will be fine-tuned for transcription using a connectionist temporal classification (CTC) loss on frame-level note labels, explicitly withholding instrument metadata during training.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a robust, general-purpose note transcription model that performs reliably across a wide spectrum of seen and unseen instruments, advancing towards domain-agnostic AMT. It will also contribute a new benchmark and analysis framework for evaluating the timbral robustness of music foundation models, extending the factual and musical evaluation metrics from prior work.
proposals.novelty: While prior work in AMT focuses on polyphonic transcription for known instruments, and self-supervised audio models like Wav2Vec learn general speech representations, this proposal uniquely targets the explicit removal of instrument identity as a necessary prior for the fundamental task of note prediction, bridging the gap between these fields.

proposals.title: GENRE: Generalized Ensemble for Robust Expressive Music Generation via Multi-Domain Dataset Curation
proposals.motivation: Current music language models (MLMs) are predominantly trained on classical piano or chamber music datasets (e.g., MAESTRO, Lakh MIDI), limiting their ability to generate stylistically diverse music. This narrow focus hinders practical applications in songwriting and creative AI, where models must adapt to varied genres, instrumentation, and harmonic vocabularies. Addressing this data bottleneck is crucial for developing truly general-purpose music AI assistants.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that systematically curating and integrating a large-scale, multi-genre symbolic music dataset will significantly improve the generalization capabilities of transformer-based music language models, leading to higher-quality and more stylistically faithful generation across jazz, pop, film, and global folk traditions.
proposals.method: We will construct the GENRE dataset by programmatically aggregating and standardizing existing symbolic corpora (e.g., Jazzomat, POP909, Wikifonia, FOLK), supplemented by new transcriptions from Hookpad's user-generated content. The dataset will be encoded using a unified tokenizer, such as REMI or the structured format from the 'sense' system. We will then train a transformer decoder model, similar to architectures used in 'aria' and 'lead', on this multi-domain corpus, employing genre and composer conditioning tokens to enable controllable generation.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver 1) the open-source GENRE dataset, a major resource for symbolic music AI, and 2) a pre-trained model demonstrating state-of-the-art multi-style generation. It will provide a rigorous evaluation of generalization in MLMs using the factual and musical metrics established in Thickstun's prior work, setting a new benchmark for the field.
proposals.novelty: While dataset expansion is a known need, the systematic curation for stylistic breadth and the integration of diverse, non-classical data sources at this scale for transformer-based MLMs is novel. The explicit focus on generalization as a measurable outcome, beyond simple scale-up, moves beyond prior single-genre model efforts.

proposals.title: Spectral Semantics: Interpreting Learned Audio Filters in Music Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: End-to-end music models like audio fingerprinters and audio-language models learn complex, frequency-selective filters directly from data. While these filters are crucial for performance, their internal representations remain opaque 'black boxes.' Interpreting them is essential for building trust, diagnosing failures, and guiding the design of more efficient and musically meaningful architectures, a core challenge in pre-2026 audio AI.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the learned convolutional filters in music foundation models correspond to interpretable musical concepts (e.g., harmonic series, percussive transients, instrument timbres) and that their activation patterns can be mapped to structural elements like onsets, chords, or melodic contours within a piece.
proposals.method: We will analyze filter banks from pre-trained models like the one from 'Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting' and components of the Hookpad Aria system. Using activation maximization and dataset examples that maximally activate specific filters, we will visualize their time-frequency responses. We will then design a probing framework, correlating filter activations with manually annotated musical features (e.g., from MIDI alignments) and with outputs from established audio analysis libraries (librosa, essentia) to quantify their semantic content.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic analysis of filter-level interpretability in music audio models. It will yield a taxonomy of learned audio features, directly linking model internals to music theory concepts. The findings will inform the development of more interpretable and controllable music generation and analysis systems.
proposals.novelty: While filter visualization is common in generic audio classification, a targeted investigation into the musical semantics of filters within state-of-the-art music-specific foundation models is novel. This bridges the gap between low-level signal processing and high-level musical understanding in end-to-end systems.

proposals.title: Polyphonic Note Detection as Object Detection in Time-Frequency Space
proposals.motivation: Automatic Music Transcription (AMT) remains a core challenge in music information retrieval, with polyphonic note detection being particularly difficult. Existing frame-wise or note-wise classification methods often struggle with precise onset/offset localization and polyphonic overlap in complex audio. A robust, generalizable solution would significantly benefit downstream tasks in music generation, analysis, and audio-language modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that framing polyphonic note detection as an object detection problem in a 2D time-frequency representation (e.g., spectrogram) will yield more accurate and temporally precise transcriptions than standard sequence-to-sequence or frame-classification approaches, by directly modeling notes as localized objects with temporal extents.
proposals.method: We will adapt a modern object detection architecture, such as YOLOv8 (pre-2026 state-of-the-art), to operate on log-mel spectrograms. Each note event will be represented as a bounding box with coordinates (onset_time, offset_time, low_freq, high_freq) and a class label (pitch). The model will be trained and evaluated on established polyphonic datasets like MAESTRO and MusicNet. We will compare performance against leading pre-2026 baselines, including Onsets and Frames models and structured output sequence models.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-performance AMT system with precise temporal localization. It will also contribute a new evaluation perspective for Music Language Models (MLMs) by offering a robust, musically-grounded transcription module for factual evaluation of generated audio, aligning with Thickstun's prior work on evaluation metrics.
proposals.novelty: While object detection is standard in computer vision, its application to note detection in spectrograms is underexplored. This approach fundamentally re-conceptualizes notes as spatial objects, differing from the dominant paradigms of autoregressive token generation or per-frame multi-label classification in pre-2026 AMT research.

proposals.title: Polyphonic Piano Transcription via Connectionist Temporal Classification with a Music Language Model Prior
proposals.motivation: Automatic music transcription (AMT) remains a core challenge in music AI, with polyphonic transcription being particularly difficult. Current systems often rely on frame-level classification or complex, multi-stage pipelines, which can be brittle and misaligned with the sequential nature of musical performance. Adapting proven sequence-to-sequence techniques from speech recognition offers a promising path toward more robust and end-to-end learnable transcription systems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a sequence-to-sequence model trained with Connectionist Temporal Classification (CTC) loss, guided by a pre-trained music language model prior, can achieve state-of-the-art polyphonic piano transcription by directly mapping audio sequences to note event sequences, overcoming alignment issues inherent in frame-based methods.
proposals.method: We will adapt the Wav2Vec 2.0 architecture, pre-trained on audio, as a feature encoder for musical audio. The encoder outputs will be fed into a Transformer decoder, trained with a CTC loss to produce a sequence of discrete note tokens (e.g., 'C4_ON', 'C4_OFF') without requiring explicit frame-wise alignment. Crucially, we will incorporate a pre-trained music language model (like the one from the 'Factual and Musical Evaluation Metrics' paper) as a prior within the decoding process, using techniques like shallow fusion to bias predictions toward musically coherent note sequences.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, end-to-end sequence modeling framework for polyphonic transcription, moving beyond standard frame-based paradigms. It will demonstrate the efficacy of CTC for musical sequence alignment and showcase how pre-trained music foundation models can be leveraged as structural priors for low-level MIR tasks, bridging the gap between audio generation and analysis.
proposals.novelty: While CTC is standard in speech, its application to polyphonic music transcription is non-trivial and underexplored. The key novelty is the integration of a music language model prior within a CTC-based transcription system, using the researcher's existing expertise in music LMs to constrain and musically regularize the transcription output.

proposals.title: Instrument-Aware Audio Embeddings via Contrastive Metric Learning for Music Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Current music foundation models, while powerful for generation and fingerprinting, often lack explicit, semantically structured representations of instrument timbre. This limits their utility for tasks requiring fine-grained control over instrumentation, such as conditional generation, source separation, or detailed musicological analysis. Learning a discriminative embedding space for instruments would provide a valuable, reusable prior for a wide range of audio and music language modeling tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying contrastive metric learning to spectrogram patches extracted from isolated instrument recordings will yield an embedding space where proximity corresponds to perceptual and acoustic similarity of timbre, and that these embeddings can improve the performance and interpretability of downstream music models.
proposals.method: We will train a convolutional encoder using a triplet loss framework on large-scale datasets of labeled, monophonic instrument sounds (e.g., NSynth). Anchor, positive (same instrument, different pitch/articulation), and negative (different instrument) examples will be formed from log-mel spectrograms. The encoder architecture will be inspired by the front-end of pre-2026 audio foundation models (e.g., contrastive predictive coding networks). We will evaluate the learned space via instrument classification, timbre similarity ranking, and by integrating the frozen encoder as a feature extractor into a downstream Music Language Model (MLLM) for an instrument-conditioned generation task.
proposals.contribution: This work will contribute a publicly available, pre-trained model for instrument-aware audio feature extraction. It will provide a quantitative framework for evaluating the timbral modeling capabilities of MLLMs, extending the factual evaluation metrics from Thickstun's prior work into the musical domain. The resulting embeddings could directly enhance tools like Hookpad Aria by enabling more precise instrument-based controls in co-writing systems.
proposals.novelty: While metric learning is established in speaker verification and image retrieval, its systematic application to learn a general-purpose, disentangled instrument timbre space for integration into music foundation models is novel. This approach moves beyond simple instrument classification to model the continuous manifold of timbral qualities.

proposals.title: Separate to Transcribe: Improving Polyphonic Note Prediction via Pre-processing with Blind Source Separation
proposals.motivation: Current polyphonic music transcription systems struggle with overlapping harmonics and complex timbres in mixed audio, leading to note prediction errors. Pre-2026, source separation models like Demucs v4 and Open-Unmix have shown strong performance in isolating individual instrument stems from music mixtures. Leveraging these advances as a pre-processing step could simplify the transcription task by providing cleaner, instrument-specific inputs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that applying state-of-the-art blind source separation to isolate primary instrument stems (e.g., vocals, bass, drums, piano) before note prediction will significantly improve the accuracy and robustness of polyphonic music transcription models.
proposals.method: We will use the open-source Demucs architecture as our primary separation model to decompose songs into stems. The isolated stems will then be used as input to a transcription model, building upon the SENSE framework for structured, event-based note prediction. We will compare the performance of this cascaded system against an end-to-end baseline on established benchmarks like MAESTRO and Slakh2100, using the factual and musical evaluation metrics from Thickstun's prior work to assess improvements in note-level precision, recall, and harmonic fidelity.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous evaluation of source separation as a pre-processing step for transcription, potentially establishing a new strong baseline for the task. It will also contribute a modular pipeline that could be integrated into music generation systems like Hookpad Aria to improve the grounding of symbolic generation in source audio.
proposals.novelty: While source separation and transcription are mature fields, their systematic combination for the explicit purpose of boosting note prediction accuracy is under-explored. The novelty lies in the targeted integration of these components and the evaluation using modern, pre-trained separation models alongside structured prediction frameworks.

proposals.title: Synthetic Audio Pre-training (SAP): Scaling Music Language Models with Perfectly Labeled Digital Instrumentation
proposals.motivation: Current Music Language Models (MLMs) are bottlenecked by the scarcity and expense of large-scale, high-quality, and accurately annotated audio datasets. Human-annotated data is limited in scale and often contains errors, while existing synthetic datasets lack the richness and diversity needed for robust model generalization. This data constraint fundamentally limits the performance and scope of MLMs in generation and understanding tasks.
proposals.hypothesis: Pre-training a transformer-based Music Language Model on a massive, procedurally generated dataset of synthetic audio, rendered with high-fidelity digital instruments and accompanied by perfect, structured symbolic labels (e.g., MIDI, note, chord, structure), will yield a foundation model with superior audio representation learning and downstream task performance compared to models trained only on natural, imperfectly labeled data.
proposals.method: We will generate a dataset of millions of synthetic audio clips by algorithmically composing musical sequences across diverse genres, structures, and complexities, rendered using high-quality soundfonts and software synthesizers (e.g., FluidSynth). Each clip will have perfectly aligned labels for notes, chords, beats, sections, and instrumentation, derived directly from the generative process. This dataset will be used to pre-train a transformer encoder-decoder model, adapting architectures like Jukebox or MusicLM but with a focus on learning from perfect alignment. The pre-trained model will then be fine-tuned and rigorously evaluated on downstream tasks including music transcription, continuation, and conditional generation using the factual and musical evaluation metrics established in Thickstun's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide: 1) a scalable methodology for creating perfectly labeled audio datasets, 2) a new, open-source pre-trained MLM demonstrating the utility of synthetic pre-training, and 3) empirical evidence on the transfer capabilities of models trained on synthetic data to natural music, advancing our understanding of audio representation learning.
proposals.novelty: While synthetic data is common in computer vision, its systematic application at scale for audio foundation model pre-training is underexplored. The key novelty is the explicit focus on leveraging perfect, multi-level label alignment during pre-training—a controlled experiment impossible with natural data—to isolate and improve the model's structural understanding of music.

proposals.title: Few-Shot Instrument Adaptation for Note Transcription via Parameter-Efficient Fine-Tuning of Audio Foundation Models
proposals.motivation: Automatic music transcription (AMT) models typically require large, instrument-specific datasets, limiting their application to rare or novel instruments. This is a critical bottleneck for ethnomusicology and creative music generation where labeled data is scarce. Pre-2026, foundation models for audio have shown remarkable generalizability, but their adaptation for structured tasks like transcription with minimal data remains underexplored.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a pre-trained audio foundation model, adapted via parameter-efficient fine-tuning (PEFT) techniques on a few minutes of labeled audio from a new instrument, can achieve accurate note transcription, significantly outperforming training from scratch or full fine-tuning in the low-data regime.
proposals.method: We will use a robust pre-trained audio encoder (e.g., a variant of the model from 'Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting') as a frozen feature extractor. For adaptation, we will attach lightweight adapters (e.g., LoRA) or prompt-tuning modules specifically to the layers responsible for pitch and temporal resolution. The system will be trained end-to-end using a connectionist temporal classification (CTC) loss on spectrogram inputs paired with sparse MIDI-like note sequences, with evaluation on a curated few-shot benchmark of diverse instruments.
proposals.contribution: This work will establish a practical framework for data-efficient AMT, directly enabling transcription for historically or culturally significant instruments. It will contribute a rigorous evaluation of PEFT methods for audio representation transfer and provide new insights into the pitch-temporal representations learned by music foundation models.
proposals.novelty: While PEFT is established in NLP and vision, its systematic application for structured audio generation tasks like transcription is novel. This approach uniquely bridges the research themes of foundation models and symbolic music generation in a low-resource setting, moving beyond prior work focused on large datasets or classification.

proposals.title: Inverting Audio Fingerprinting: Neural Synthesis of Polyphonic Audio from Symbolic Note Sequences
proposals.motivation: Current music generation models often operate in a single modality, either symbolic (MIDI) or audio. A robust method to synthesize realistic polyphonic audio directly from a sequence of discrete note and instrument labels would bridge this gap, enabling powerful applications in music production, interactive composition tools, and data augmentation for audio analysis. This addresses a key inverse problem in music information retrieval, complementing the forward task of audio fingerprinting and transcription.
proposals.hypothesis: A neural audio generation model, trained by inverting the feature extraction process of a robust audio fingerprinting model (like those used in pre-2026 foundation models), can synthesize perceptually convincing and instrument-specific audio from symbolic note sequences without requiring paired MIDI-audio datasets.
proposals.method: We will leverage the encoder of a pre-trained, robust neural audio fingerprinting model (e.g., a variant of the system from 'Robust Neural Audio Fingerprinting using Music Foundation Models') as a fixed feature extractor. A conditional diffusion model or adversarial autoencoder will be trained to generate mel-spectrograms from input sequences of (pitch, onset, offset, instrument) tokens, using the fingerprint model's embeddings as a reconstruction target and perceptual loss. The synthesized spectrograms will then be converted to waveform using a pre-trained neural vocoder (e.g., HiFi-GAN). The training can utilize large-scale, unlabeled audio corpora by generating pseudo-symbolic labels via an off-the-shelf transcription model.
proposals.contribution: This work will introduce a novel paradigm for conditional audio synthesis that is disentangled from specific audio-text pairs, advancing controllable music generation. It will provide a public model and benchmark for the 'audio-from-notes' task, filling a gap between symbolic AI and audio synthesis. The methodology could also offer insights into the representations learned by audio foundation models.
proposals.novelty: Unlike prior audio synthesis from MIDI (e.g., Google's MIDI-DDPM), this approach does not require aligned symbolic-audio data for training, instead using a pre-trained fingerprint model as an implicit, high-quality audio prior. It frames the problem explicitly as inverting a known, robust analysis model, a direction less explored than end-to-end conditional generation.

proposals.title: Real-Time Music Language Modeling: Architectural Optimizations for Low-Latency Note Prediction on Edge Devices
proposals.motivation: Current Music Language Models (MLMs) are large autoregressive models requiring significant compute, making them unsuitable for real-time, interactive applications like live performance aids or educational tools on constrained devices. The push towards on-device AI for latency, privacy, and accessibility necessitates a new class of efficient audio-generation models. This work addresses the gap between high-quality symbolic music generation and the strict computational limits of real-time prediction.
proposals.hypothesis: By applying a combination of architectural pruning, knowledge distillation from large pre-trained MLMs, and causal, sub-second context modeling, we can develop a compact model that achieves real-time note prediction with minimal quality loss compared to state-of-the-art, non-real-time models.
proposals.method: We will adapt a small-scale Transformer decoder (e.g., 6-layer, 4-head) as a baseline. The core method involves: 1) Knowledge Distillation from a large pre-trained MLM (like the one used in 'Hookpad Aria') to transfer musical knowledge, 2) Structured Pruning of attention heads and feed-forward layers based on activation patterns, and 3) Implementing a strict causal, rolling-window input of the past 1-2 seconds of symbolic notes (e.g., MIDI events) to enforce fixed, low-latency inference. Training will use a next-token prediction objective on symbolic datasets (e.g., Lakh MIDI), with evaluation via the factual and musical metrics from Thickstun's prior work.
proposals.contribution: This project will deliver a publicly available, lightweight MLM capable of running in real-time on consumer hardware (e.g., laptops, tablets), enabling new interactive music applications. It will provide a framework and benchmark for evaluating the trade-offs between model size, latency, and musical quality in autoregressive audio tasks.
proposals.novelty: While model compression is well-studied in NLP and vision, its targeted application to real-time symbolic music prediction, combined with distillation from large musical foundation models, is unexplored. This work moves beyond offline music generation to optimize specifically for the latency and resource constraints of interactive use.

ideas.title: Structure-Grounded Hybrid Music Representation Learning
ideas.description: This approach learns a dual representation for music: a discrete, grammar-aware symbolic stream and a continuous, performance-nuance stream. A novel consistency objective forces the continuous features to align with the structural abstractions captured by the discrete tokenizer, preventing semantic collapse. This ensures the model generates outputs that are both expressively nuanced and structurally coherent, effectively bridging the gap between raw audio modeling and musical intelligence.

ideas.title: Distortion-Free Watermarking for Non-Autoregressive Models with Learned Continuous Representations
ideas.description: This research develops a watermarking framework specifically for non-autoregressive generative models that use learned, continuous internal representations, such as diffusion models or flow-based architectures for audio or symbolic music. The core challenge is to embed a statistical signal for detection without distorting the model's output, despite the lack of a sequential, discrete token space where traditional watermarks are applied. The solution involves operating the watermarking mechanism within the model's learned latent or continuous feature space, leveraging its inherent structure to insert a detectable yet imperceptible signature during the parallel generation process. This creates a new class of watermarking tools for fast, high-fidelity generative models in media domains beyond text, ensuring provenance without sacrificing quality or generation speed.

ideas.title: Inverse Control Attribution for Non-Autoregressive Generative Models
ideas.description: This research develops a method to infer the latent control parameters that guided the generation of a complete piece of music or text from a non-autoregressive model. By adapting principles from distortion-free watermarking, the approach embeds a recoverable signature within the control process itself, rather than the output tokens. This allows for the extraction and verification of the original generative intent or guiding constraints, providing a new tool for authorship attribution and detecting undisclosed conditioning in model outputs. The synergy lies in solving the inverse problem in a verifiable way for architectures where sequential dependency cannot be assumed.

ideas.title: Program Synthesis for Music Generation with Integrated Neural Aesthetic Guidance
ideas.description: This approach combines the structured control of program synthesis with data-driven aesthetic guidance to generate musically compelling outputs. A synthesis engine proposes candidate symbolic programs that generate musical sequences, which are then evaluated by a neural network trained as an aesthetic critic on human judgments and expressive performances. The critic's feedback is used to guide the search towards programs that produce music with desirable qualities like natural phrasing, dynamic variation, and idiomatic expression. This results in a hybrid system that offers the explicit controllability of programs while ensuring the generated music meets a high standard of musicality and listenability.

ideas.description: This research develops a compact neural model for music anticipation on edge devices, explicitly engineered to overcome catastrophic forgetting and adapt in real-time. The system combines a small-footprint core predictor with an efficient on-device rehearsal mechanism and elastic weight consolidation, enabling it to learn continuously from shifting user behavior and acoustic contexts without requiring cloud retraining. This results in a robust, personalized model that maintains performance over time while operating entirely under the constraints of mobile or embedded hardware, ensuring privacy and responsiveness.

ideas.title: Disentangled Adversarial Panel for Robust Multi-Aspect Optimization
ideas.description: This system replaces the single discriminator with a panel of disentangled, modular critic networks, each trained to evaluate a specific, well-defined dimension of output quality such as factual grounding, instruction following, or stylistic integrity. A meta-controller dynamically adjusts the influence of each critic on the generator's loss based on real-time performance audits, preventing the generator from gaming any single metric. This forces the generator to holistically improve across all desired attributes, leading to more robust and controllable anticipatory generation. The modular design also allows for the transparent diagnosis of failure modes and the incremental addition of new quality dimensions.

ideas.title: Unified Bidirectional Generation via Anchor Set Completion
ideas.description: This research proposes and tests a model architecture trained exclusively for the task of completing a full sequence from a sparse, unordered set of provided 'anchor' tokens. The training objective is a consistent masked generation loss over all non-anchor positions, using a non-causal transformer to process the entire sequence bidirectionally. This forces the model to develop robust planning and integration capabilities, as infilling (where anchors are interior tokens) and standard generation (where anchors are a prefix) become specific instances of the same anchor-set completion task. The approach provides a concrete, measurable test of whether a unified generation model can match or exceed the performance of task-specialized models across the full spectrum of generation tasks.

ideas.title: Task-Grounded Robustness to Semantic-Preserving Transformations via Human-AI Adversarial Frontiers
ideas.description: This research addresses the fundamental ambiguity in 'semantic-preserving' by grounding robustness in concrete task performance and human evaluation. The system is trained to maintain output consistency within defined bounds when inputs undergo transformations like translation or summarization, where the bounds are empirically set by measuring acceptable performance loss on end tasks (e.g., QA, sentiment). An adversarial human-in-the-loop component iteratively generates and filters transformation examples that cause failures, using this data to expand the model's robust frontier. The result is a practically measurable and improvable form of robustness, moving beyond a brittle, all-or-nothing definition.

ideas.title: Continuous-Time Watermarking of Neural Dynamical Systems
ideas.description: This research proposes using neural ordinary differential equations (ODEs) to model the continuous-time dynamics of a generative system's latent state. A coherent watermark is embedded not in discrete outputs, but as a structured perturbation within the ODE's vector field. This ensures that every generated sample in a temporally extended sequence—whether text, code, or other sequential data—inherits a mathematically traceable signature from the system's initial conditions and dynamics. The approach enables robust, post-hoc attribution of long-horizon generative content to its source model, even after arbitrary interpolation or editing in the continuous-time latent space, by verifying the consistency of the watermark across the entire output trajectory.

ideas.title: Joint Watermarking for Lightweight, Multi-Output Anticipatory Models on Edge Devices
ideas.description: This research fuses the development of compact anticipatory models for edge devices with joint watermarking techniques to create a novel framework for secure, efficient generative AI on resource-constrained hardware. The core innovation is a lightweight model architecture capable of producing a sequence of related outputs (e.g., the next few notes in a melody, or a series of predictive text tokens) and embedding a single, coherent digital watermark across the entire sequence jointly, rather than in each output independently. This approach drastically reduces the per-output computational and memory overhead of watermarking, making it feasible for real-time applications on mobile or embedded systems. The joint watermark acts as a unified cryptographic seal, allowing for the later verification of the entire generative sequence's origin and integrity, addressing both deployment efficiency and content security in a single system.

ideas.title: Publicly Verifiable Hierarchical Planning for Generative Model Provenance
ideas.description: This research fuses hierarchical reinforcement learning (HRL) planning with cryptographic signatures to create a new paradigm for transparent and verifiable generative AI. A generative model, such as one for music, uses a high-level planning module to produce a verifiable 'structural blueprint' (e.g., a sequence of intended sections, key changes, or thematic developments) before generating the final low-level sequence. This high-level plan is then signed using a digital signature scheme derived from the model's internal state or weights, creating an immutable, publicly verifiable attestation of the model's creative intent. The synergy enables auditors or users to verify that a generated artifact (like a song) not only originated from a specific model but was also produced according to an authentic, unaltered compositional strategy, providing unprecedented insight into the model's decision-making provenance.

ideas.title: Apply Diffusion Models for Anticipatory Infilling via Protein Folding Analogy
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from protein structure prediction, specifically AlphaFold2's refinement process, to the problem of anticipatory infilling in interleaved event-control sequences. In the analogous field, a noisy 3D protein structure is iteratively refined into a precise model using deep learning conditioned on evolutionary and physical constraints. The adapted research idea applies this paradigm by using a diffusion model to treat a sequence of future events and required control actions as a structure to be denoised. Starting from a noisy or masked future segment, the model would perform conditional generation, iteratively refining the sequence to be coherent with the observed past events and specified control objectives, effectively 'folding' a plausible future trajectory from a prior distribution.

ideas.title: Adversarial Co-Evolution of Dynamic Control and Event Generation
ideas.description: This research proposes a framework where a generator (event process) and a controller are both dynamic neural modules, co-trained adversarially against a discriminator. The discriminator's role is dual: it must judge the realism of generated event sequences while also evaluating how well they adhere to the dynamically produced control signals. This adversarial pressure forces the controller to learn to produce control processes that are both effective for guiding the generator and inherently learnable, while the generator learns to respond flexibly to evolving control. The result is a system where the control mechanism and the generative model co-evolve, discovering novel and efficient control-generation partnerships that would not be possible with a fixed control scheme or independent training.

ideas.title: Adversarial Co-Evolution of Generator and Discriminator via a Predictive Fitness Landscape
ideas.description: This idea expands the original seed of joint generation-discrimination training by drawing an analogy from evolutionary biology and game theory, specifically predator-prey arms races. The analogous solution is a co-evolutionary system where the fitness landscape for one agent is defined by the evolving strategy of its adversary, creating perpetual, scaling improvement. Adapted to the research context, this involves co-training the anticipatory generator with a discriminator that is itself trained to predict the generator's emerging flaws. The two models engage in a closed-loop, adversarial co-evolution: the discriminator learns to anticipate and critique the generator's next-level failures, while the generator is trained to fulfill its core tasks while evading this increasingly sophisticated criticism. This dynamic creates an auto-scaling difficulty mechanism, ensuring the generator's output quality and control adherence are continuously pressure-tested and improved against an adaptive benchmark, preventing plateauing and promoting robust generalization.

ideas.title: Information-Constrained Joint Learning of Control and Event Processes
ideas.description: This approach jointly learns a dynamic control process alongside the event process but explicitly guards against the degenerate solution where the control process becomes uninformative. It does so by formulating a training objective that includes a lower bound on the mutual information between the control trajectory and the event history, ensuring the control process captures meaningful temporal structure. The framework also incorporates a regularization term to promote stability in the generated control signals. This results in a model where the adaptive control process is both non-trivial and practically useful for improving event prediction and understanding.

ideas.title: Latent Anticipatory Control via Self-Supervised Forward Model Discrepancy
ideas.description: This research develops a method for anticipatory training in generic control tasks by learning a latent space where anticipation is well-defined. A key component is a forward model trained to predict future latent states from current states and actions. The policy is then trained not only to maximize task reward but also to take actions that produce a measurable discrepancy between its own anticipated latent future and the forward model's prediction, creating a dense, self-supervised learning signal. This approach provides a concrete, learned definition of 'anticipation' for any control space, enabling improved exploration and policy robustness in tasks with delayed or sparse rewards.

ideas.title: Diffusion-Based Anticipatory Planning for General Control Spaces
ideas.description: This research direction combines the anticipatory training framework for general control tasks with the generative power of diffusion models. It proposes training a diffusion model to operate directly in a unified sequence space that interleaves future environmental events and the agent's control actions, where controls are not a subset of events but a separate, interacting modality. The model learns to iteratively denoise a trajectory, starting from pure noise, to produce sequences where control actions are optimally aligned with anticipated future states, even for complex, high-dimensional control spaces like torque vectors or continuous parameters. This enables a new form of flexible, model-based planning that can generate diverse and probabilistically sound long-horizon behavior policies from a single learned generative process.

ideas.title: Adversarially-Trained Feature-Space Watermarking with Non-Linear Erasure Codes
ideas.description: This system integrates adversarial training, deep feature-space embedding, and advanced channel coding to create watermarks that are robust against both random corruption and targeted removal attacks. A neural encoder embeds the watermark into perceptually significant, high-dimensional features of the host media, making removal without severe quality loss difficult. The payload is encoded using non-linear, rateless error-correcting codes optimized for extreme erasure scenarios, providing recovery from massive, non-random data loss. The entire pipeline is trained end-to-end against a suite of adversarial corruption models, ensuring the discovered embedding and code strategies are inherently resilient to the strongest known attacks.

ideas.title: Bidirectional Predictive Coupling via Mutual Information Minimization
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from computational neuroscience's predictive coding theory to the challenge of bidirectional anticipation between processes. In predictive coding, neural hierarchies solve the analogous problem of interactive anticipation through bidirectional message passing that minimizes prediction error (or mutual information). The adapted research idea implements this by having both the event and control processes maintain lightweight, adaptive generative models of each other. They continuously exchange predictions and compare them to actual signals, using the resulting 'prediction errors' to dynamically adjust their own actions and update their internal models of the other process. This creates a closed-loop system where anticipation is mutual, interactive, and co-adaptive, fundamentally challenging the assumption that anticipation must be unidirectional or that processes have fixed, independent models of each other.

ideas.title: Amortized Anticipatory Modeling for Continuous-Time Point Processes via Latent State Evolution
ideas.description: This adversarially improved system overcomes the computational intractability of explicit rollouts in continuous time by learning a deterministic or stochastic latent process that evolves between observed events. This latent state is trained to implicitly contain the information needed for anticipation, allowing the model to predict the intensity and mark distribution for future events in constant time. The approach leverages neural ordinary differential equations or continuous-time flow networks to model the smooth evolution of anticipation. This enables scalable, real-time anticipatory modeling for applications like network monitoring, medical event forecasting, and financial transaction modeling.

ideas.title: Anticipatory Robustness via Bidirectional Process Alignment
ideas.description: This research direction develops models where the event-generation process and the control/planning process bidirectionally anticipate each other's outputs and constraints. This interactive anticipation is explicitly trained to be invariant under a broad spectrum of semantic-preserving transformations, such as translation, summarization, and paraphrasing. Consequently, the model learns to maintain coherent, goal-directed behavior not by merely resisting perturbations but by having its internal processes actively coordinate and self-correct across different surface forms of the same meaning. This leads to a new form of robustness grounded in the dynamic alignment of internal reasoning states, generalizing beyond robustness to simple edits or static representations.

ideas.title: Temporally-Constrained Diffusion for Causal Anticipatory Infilling
ideas.description: This system combines a diffusion model for generative flexibility with a symbolic temporal logic engine to enforce global consistency. During the reverse denoising process, candidate infills are projected onto a manifold of sequences that satisfy hard temporal and causal constraints, such as event precedence and resource continuity. This neuro-symbolic approach ensures the generated interleaved event-control sequences are not only locally plausible but also globally coherent and causally valid. The result is a robust anticipatory infilling model that mitigates the risk of compounding hallucinated errors.

ideas.title: Adapting Predictive Control from Neuroscience for Continuous-Time Anticipatory Models
ideas.description: This research seed proposes extending anticipatory modeling from discrete symbolic sequences to continuous-time temporal point processes. Drawing an analogy from computational neuroscience, specifically the forward-model predictive control framework used in motor control, we adapt its core solution. In motor control, the brain uses an internal forward model to continuously predict the sensory outcome of actions, enabling anticipatory corrections. We adapt this by developing a model for continuous-time processes that maintains a real-time, evolving prediction of the process state or intensity function. This continuous prediction is compared against a learned target trajectory, producing a continuous error signal. This signal is then fed back to adaptively modulate the underlying point process dynamics, allowing the system to anticipate and smoothly compensate for deviations, rather than just reacting to discrete event predictions. This enables more nuanced and temporally precise anticipatory behavior in domains like network traffic modeling, financial transactions, or medical event monitoring.

ideas.title: Invariant Feature Learning via Topological Data Analysis for Semantic Transformations
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that robustness should be built against local, token-level edits by proposing a method inspired by Topological Data Analysis. In TDA, persistent homology identifies robust, multi-scale shape features invariant to continuous deformation. Adapted to NLP, we propose to model the semantic space of textual meaning as a topological space constructed from embeddings. The goal is to train models to identify and rely on 'persistent semantic features'—the stable topological structure of the meaning, such as the core narrative or logical relationships—that endure across high-level, semantic-preserving transformations like translation, summarization, or extensive paraphrasing. This shifts the robustness paradigm from defending against point-wise perturbations to learning representations anchored to the invariant topological skeleton of the meaning itself.

ideas.title: Anticipatory Steganography for Robust Continuous-Time Process Watermarking
ideas.description: This research fuses steganographic error-correcting codes with anticipatory modeling of continuous-time temporal point processes to develop a novel watermarking framework for dynamic event streams. The core innovation is to use the anticipatory model—which predicts future event timings and types—to inform and optimize the placement and redundancy of the steganographic code within the observed event sequence. This allows the watermark to be embedded in a manner that is minimally disruptive to the natural process statistics while being maximally resilient to both the inherent stochasticity of the process and external adversarial or non-adversarial corruption. Consequently, the watermark becomes an integral, adaptive component of the process dynamics, enabling reliable authentication and tracing of data like financial transactions, network logs, or biological spike trains even under heavy distortion.

ideas.title: Robust Causal Discovery for Event-Control Systems with Latent Confounders and Non-Stationarity
ideas.description: This research develops a robust causal discovery framework specifically designed for learning dependencies between event and control processes under real-world conditions. The system explicitly addresses the fatal flaws of latent confounding and non-stationary dynamics by integrating instrumental variable detection, change-point analysis for regime identification, and an active testing protocol that leverages existing control mechanisms for targeted interventions. The output is a validated, time-varying causal graph that identifies both direct relationships and the influence of unobserved variables, providing a reliable model for process understanding and optimization.

ideas.title: Context-Keyed Dynamic Watermarking for Multi-Output Provenance
ideas.description: This system embeds distinct but verifiably linked watermarks across separate model outputs by generating each watermark as a function of a master secret key and a robust semantic hash of the individual output's content. This approach ensures that while the verifier can cryptographically demonstrate that all texts originated from the same source, an adversary analyzing multiple watermarked texts cannot find a consistent, separable watermark pattern, as each mark is unique and content-dependent. The method maintains robustness against removal attacks, as altering the text to erase the watermark significantly degrades quality, and provides strong collusion resistance. It thus enables secure, stealthy joint provenance tracking without introducing a detectable joint statistical artifact.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery for Robust, Distribution-Preserving Watermarking Under Adaptive Attacks
ideas.description: This research direction integrates causal discovery with distribution-preserving watermarking to defend against adaptive adversaries. It applies causal inference to learn the dependency structure between the watermarking mechanism (control process) and the attacker's query-adaptation strategy (event process). By identifying these causal links, the watermark can be designed to be interventionally robust—ensuring the output distribution remains unchanged even when the attacker performs chosen-query or model-fine-tuning attacks. This results in a watermarking scheme that is not only statistically undetectable but also maintains its distortion-freeness by being causally insulated from the attacker's adaptive actions.

ideas.title: Zero-Knowledge Watermarking via Publicly Verifiable Statistical Artifacts
ideas.description: This research adapts the forensic ballistics approach to create a watermarking scheme that requires no shared secret key. The core idea is to treat the generative model like a specific firearm. During its training or fine-tuning, a unique, publicly known constraint is applied (analogous to a rifling pattern), which systematically biases its output distribution in a subtle, statistically measurable way. This creates a class-characteristic 'fingerprint'. The verifier, possessing only a public identifier for the model in question, can challenge it to produce a fresh 'test pattern' (a set of outputs under controlled conditions). The verifier then uses statistical hypothesis testing on the suspected content to check for the presence of this specific fingerprint pattern, matching it against the freshly generated reference. This proves the content originated from that specific model without any prior secret key exchange, challenging the assumption that watermark detection necessitates symmetric cryptography or pre-distributed secrets.

ideas.title: Causal Discovery of Interactive Anticipatory Feedback Loops Between Processes
ideas.description: This research direction integrates causal discovery with interactive anticipation to learn not just if processes influence each other, but *how* they anticipate each other's future states in a closed loop. It applies time-series causal inference methods (e.g., based on conditional independence or noise asymmetry) to observational data of the interacting event and control processes, explicitly testing for and modeling bidirectional, time-lagged predictive relationships. The novel synergy is a causal model that distinguishes between reactive influence and anticipatory influence, revealing whether the processes engage in a predictive feedback loop that could lead to complex emergent dynamics like synchronization or destabilization, which neither dependency learning nor pure anticipation modeling alone could characterize.

ideas.title: Publicly Verifiable, Keyless Watermarking via Digital Signatures
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes cryptographic signatures and keyless watermarking to create a unified provenance and authenticity framework. A generative model is equipped with a private key to cryptographically sign a unique, model-derived watermark pattern embedded into its outputs. Any verifier can then use the corresponding public key to confirm the model's identity and, simultaneously, detect the presence of the signed watermark within the content itself. This enables open-world, trust-on-first-use verification where neither the watermarking secret nor the verification algorithm needs to be distributed confidentially in advance.

ideas.title: Adapting Causal Discovery from Systems Biology to Learn Process Dependencies
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to the analogous problem of gene regulatory network inference in systems biology. In that field, causal discovery methods like dynamic Bayesian networks and constraint-based algorithms are applied to time-series expression data under perturbations to learn the directed dependency structure between genes. We adapt this by treating event logs and control system telemetry as analogous multivariate time-series data, where system interventions (e.g., manual commands, fault injections) serve as natural experiments. Applying these causal inference methods will allow us to learn the underlying causal graph between event and control processes, moving beyond correlation to identify the true directional dependencies and interaction mechanisms.

ideas.title: Anticipatory Control via Predictive State Representation, Inspired by Ecological Niche Modeling
ideas.description: The original idea seeks to apply anticipatory methods to control tasks where controls are not a subset of events, making direct future prediction of controls infeasible. Drawing an analogy from ecological niche modeling, where species distributions are predicted based on anticipated future environmental states rather than the mechanisms of migration, we propose a two-stage anticipatory control architecture. First, a predictive model is trained to learn a compact, task-relevant representation of the system's future state (the 'anticipatory state'), analogous to a future habitat suitability map. Second, a control policy is trained to take actions based on this anticipatory state representation, enabling the controller to act in the present based on a distilled prediction of future consequences, effectively solving the non-infilling problem by separating anticipation from action selection.

ideas.title: Analyzing Watermark Decay and Failure Modes in Unbounded Generation via Cryptographic Key Exhaustion Models
ideas.description: This research adapts solution methods from cryptography, specifically the analysis of pseudorandom number generators and stream ciphers under key exhaustion or state reuse. The analogous problem is that cryptographic guarantees (like indistinguishability from randomness) break down in a quantifiable way when a generator is operated beyond its designed period or with a repeated state. The solution involves modeling entropy depletion and defining failure bounds for security properties. Adapted to watermarking for LLMs, this involves providing a formal analysis of watermark properties—such as detectability, robustness, and unforgeability—when text generation continues far beyond the protocol's stated maximum budget. The core idea is to model the watermark's internal stochastic process as a finite resource that decays, deriving theoretical bounds on how the guarantees degrade (e.g., the adversary's advantage in removing or forging the mark) and potentially defining a 'security half-life' or failure modes for unbounded generation, moving beyond the binary of 'within budget' vs. 'broken.'

ideas.title: Formal Security Against Key Reconstruction in Unbounded Watermarking with Dynamic Schemes
ideas.description: This research provides a formal cryptographic security analysis for watermarking schemes under the threat of key reconstruction attacks via unlimited generation. It defines a security game where an adaptive adversary with a black-box query budget attempts to break the watermark (e.g., forge unwatermarked content or evade detection). The core contribution is the design and analysis of a dynamic, stateful watermarking protocol where the effective watermark key evolves, provably limiting the information leakage per query. The work establishes new theoretical bounds on the adversary's advantage as a function of their query budget, proving that reconstruction becomes computationally infeasible even for unbounded generation, thereby strengthening the practical guarantees of watermarks.

An event-conditioned jump process dynamically updates the SDE's state and parameters upon receiving asynchronous, potentially irregular observations, ensuring robustness to missing data. This hybrid continuous-discrete formulation enables accurate anticipation of future states and optimal control actions in noisy, real-world environments where event timing and data quality are unreliable.
